The **European Union** (**EU**) is a [political](/wiki/Political_union "Political union") and [economic union](/wiki/Economic_union "Economic union") of 27 [member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") that are [located primarily](/wiki/Geography_of_the_European_Union "Geography of the European Union") in Europe.[[8]](#cite_note-14)[[9]](#cite_note-15) The [supranational union](/wiki/Supranational_union "Supranational union") has a total area of 4,233,255 km2 (1,634,469 sq mi) and an estimated population of more than 450 million as of 2025. The EU is often described as a *[sui generis](/wiki/Sui_generis "Sui generis")* political entity combining characteristics of both a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation") and a [confederation](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation").[[10]](#cite_note-16)[[11]](#cite_note-17)

Containing 5.5% of the [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population") in 2023,[[12]](#cite_note-18) EU member states generated a nominal [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) of around €17.935 trillion in 2024, accounting for approximately one sixth of [global economic output](/wiki/Global_economic_output "Global economic output").[[13]](#cite_note-19) Its cornerstone, the [Customs Union](/wiki/European_Union_Customs_Union "European Union Customs Union"), paved the way to establishing [an internal single market](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market") based on standardised [legal framework and legislation](/wiki/European_Union_law "European Union law") that applies in all member states in those matters, and only those matters, where the states have agreed to act as one. EU policies aim to ensure the free movement of people, goods, services and capital within the internal market;[[14]](#cite_note-Europa_Internal_Market-20) enact legislation in justice and home affairs; and maintain common policies on [trade](/wiki/Common_Commercial_Policy_(EU) "Common Commercial Policy (EU)"),[[15]](#cite_note-21) [agriculture](/wiki/Common_Agricultural_Policy "Common Agricultural Policy"),[[16]](#cite_note-22) [fisheries](/wiki/Common_Fisheries_Policy "Common Fisheries Policy"), and [regional development](/wiki/Regional_policy_of_the_European_Union "Regional policy of the European Union").[[17]](#cite_note-23)

Passport controls have been abolished for travel within the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area").[[18]](#cite_note-Internal_borders-24) The [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") is a group composed of the 21 EU member states that have fully implemented the [EU's economic and monetary union](/wiki/Economic_and_Monetary_Union_of_the_European_Union "Economic and Monetary Union of the European Union") and use the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") currency. Through the [Common Foreign and Security Policy](/wiki/Common_Foreign_and_Security_Policy "Common Foreign and Security Policy"), the union has developed [a role in external relations](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_European_Union "Foreign relations of the European Union") and [defence](/wiki/Military_of_the_European_Union "Military of the European Union"). It maintains permanent [diplomatic missions](/wiki/Diplomatic_missions_of_the_European_Union "Diplomatic missions of the European Union") throughout the world and represents itself at the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), the [G7](/wiki/European_Union_and_the_G7 "European Union and the G7") and the [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20"). In 2012, the EU [was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/2012_Nobel_Peace_Prize "2012 Nobel Peace Prize").

The EU was established, along with [its citizenship](/wiki/European_Union_citizenship "European Union citizenship"), when the [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty") came into force in 1993, and was incorporated as an [international legal](/wiki/International_law "International law") [juridical person](/wiki/Juridical_person "Juridical person")[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] upon entry into force of the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") in 2009.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraigDe_Burca201115-25) Its beginnings can be traced to the [Inner Six](/wiki/Inner_Six "Inner Six") states (Belgium, France, Italy, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, and [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany")) at the start of modern [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") in 1948, and to the [Western Union](/wiki/Western_Union_(alliance) "Western Union (alliance)"), the [International Authority for the Ruhr](/wiki/International_Authority_for_the_Ruhr "International Authority for the Ruhr"), the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community"), the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") and the [European Atomic Energy Community](/wiki/European_Atomic_Energy_Community "European Atomic Energy Community"), which were established by treaties. These increasingly amalgamated bodies grew, with their legal successor the EU, both in size through [the accessions of a further 22 states](/wiki/Enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Enlargement of the European Union") from 1973 to 2013, and in power through acquisitions of policy areas. In 2020, [the United Kingdom became](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") the only member state to [leave the EU](/wiki/Withdrawal_from_the_European_Union "Withdrawal from the European Union"); ten countries are [aspiring or negotiating to join it](/wiki/Potential_enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Potential enlargement of the European Union").

### Background: World Wars and aftermath

Further information: [Ideas of European unity before 1948](/wiki/Ideas_of_European_unity_before_1948 "Ideas of European unity before 1948")

[Internationalism](/wiki/Internationalism_(politics) "Internationalism (politics)") and [visions of European unity had existed](/wiki/Ideas_of_European_unity_before_1948 "Ideas of European unity before 1948") since well before the 19th century, but gained particularly as a reaction to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and its aftermath. In this light the first advances for the idea of [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") were made. In 1920 [John Maynard Keynes](/wiki/John_Maynard_Keynes "John Maynard Keynes") proposed a [European customs union](/wiki/European_customs_union "European customs union") for the struggling post-war European economies,[[20]](#cite_note-Taussig_Keynes_1920_p._381-26) and in 1923 the oldest organisation for European integration, the [Paneuropean Union](/wiki/Paneuropean_Union "Paneuropean Union") was founded, led by [Richard von Coudenhove-Kalergi](/wiki/Richard_von_Coudenhove-Kalergi "Richard von Coudenhove-Kalergi"), who later would found in June 1947 the [European Parliamentary Union](/wiki/European_Parliamentary_Union "European Parliamentary Union") (EPU). [Aristide Briand](/wiki/Aristide_Briand "Aristide Briand")—who was [Prime Minister of France](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_France "Prime Minister of France"), a follower of the Paneuropean Union, and [Nobel Peace Prize laureate](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_Peace_Prize_laureates "List of Nobel Peace Prize laureates") for the [Locarno Treaties](/wiki/Locarno_Treaties "Locarno Treaties")—delivered a widely recognised speech at the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") in [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva") on 5 September 1929 for a [federal Europe](/wiki/Federal_Europe "Federal Europe") to secure Europe and settle the historic [Franco-German enmity](/wiki/Franco-German_enmity "Franco-German enmity").[[21]](#cite_note-Schulz_2010_g046-27)[[22]](#cite_note-Nelsson_2019_n549-28)

With large-scale war being waged in Europe once again in the 1930s and becoming [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the question of what to fight against and what for had to be agreed on. A first agreement was the [Declaration of St James's Palace](/wiki/Declaration_of_St_James%27s_Palace "Declaration of St James's Palace") of 1941, when [Europe's resistance](/wiki/World_War_II_resistance "World War II resistance") gathered in London. This was expanded on by the 1941 [Atlantic Charter](/wiki/Atlantic_Charter "Atlantic Charter"), establishing the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") and their common goals, inciting a new wave of global international institutions like the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") ([founded 1945](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_International_Organization "United Nations Conference on International Organization")) or the [Bretton Woods System](/wiki/Bretton_Woods_System "Bretton Woods System") (1944).[[23]](#cite_note-Office_of_the_Historian_1946_r351-29)

In 1943 at the [Moscow Conference](/wiki/Moscow_Conference_(1943) "Moscow Conference (1943)") and [Tehran Conference](/wiki/Tehran_Conference "Tehran Conference"), plans to establish joint institutions for a post-war world and Europe increasingly became a part of the agenda. This led to a decision at the [Yalta Conference](/wiki/Yalta_Conference "Yalta Conference") in 1944 to form a [European Advisory Commission](/wiki/European_Advisory_Commission "European Advisory Commission"), later replaced by the [Council of Foreign Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Foreign_Ministers "Council of Foreign Ministers") and the [Allied Control Council](/wiki/Allied_Control_Council "Allied Control Council"), following the German surrender and the [Potsdam Agreement](/wiki/Potsdam_Agreement "Potsdam Agreement") in 1945.

By the end of the war, European integration became seen as an antidote to the [extreme nationalism](/wiki/Extreme_nationalism "Extreme nationalism") that had caused the war.[[24]](#cite_note-30) On 19 September 1946, in a much recognised speech, [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), speaking at the [University of Zürich](/wiki/University_of_Z%C3%BCrich "University of Zürich"), reiterated his calls since 1930 for a "European Union" and "Council of Europe", coincidentally[[25]](#cite_note-Union_of_European_Federalists_(UEF)-31) parallel[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] to the [Hertenstein Congress](/wiki/Hertensteiner_Cross "Hertensteiner Cross") of the [Union of European Federalists](/wiki/Union_of_European_Federalists "Union of European Federalists"),[[26]](#cite_note-32) one of the then founded and later constituent members of the [European Movement](/wiki/European_Movement_International "European Movement International"). One month later, the [French Union](/wiki/French_Union "French Union") was installed by the new [Fourth French Republic](/wiki/Fourth_French_Republic "Fourth French Republic") to direct the [decolonisation](/wiki/Decolonization "Decolonization") of [its colonies](/wiki/French_colonies "French colonies") so that they would become parts of a European community.[[27]](#cite_note-33)

By 1947, a growing rift between the western Allied Powers and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") became evident as a result of the rigged [1947 Polish legislative election](/wiki/1947_Polish_legislative_election "1947 Polish legislative election"), which constituted an open breach of the [Yalta Agreement](/wiki/Yalta_Agreement "Yalta Agreement"). March of that year saw two important developments. First was the signing of the [Treaty of Dunkirk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Dunkirk "Treaty of Dunkirk") between [France](/wiki/France "France") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The treaty assured mutual assistance in the event of future military aggression against either nation. Though it officially named Germany as a threat, in reality the actual concern was for the Soviet Union. A few days later came the announcement of the [Truman Doctrine](/wiki/Truman_Doctrine "Truman Doctrine") which pledged American support for democracies to counter the Soviets.

### Initial years and the Paris Treaty (1948‍–‍1957)

Main article: [History of European integration (1948–1957)](/wiki/History_of_European_integration_(1948%E2%80%931957) "History of European integration (1948–1957)")

An excerpt of the [Schuman Declaration](/wiki/Schuman_Declaration "Schuman Declaration"), by [Robert Schuman](/wiki/Robert_Schuman "Robert Schuman") on 9 May 1950 ([Europe Day](/wiki/Europe_Day "Europe Day"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/1951_CECA_ECSC.jpg/250px-1951_CECA_ECSC.jpg)](/wiki/File:1951_CECA_ECSC.jpg)

[Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1951) "Treaty of Paris (1951)") (1951), establishing the [ECSC](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community")

Immediately following the [February 1948 coup d'état](/wiki/1948_Czechoslovak_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1948 Czechoslovak coup d'état") by the [Communist Party of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Czechoslovakia "Communist Party of Czechoslovakia"), the [London Six-Power Conference](/wiki/London_Six-Power_Conference "London Six-Power Conference") was held, resulting in the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") boycott of the Allied Control Council and its incapacitation, an event marking the beginning of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). The year 1948 marked the beginning of the institutionalised modern [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration"). In March 1948 the [Treaty of Brussels](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brussels "Treaty of Brussels") was signed, establishing the [Western Union](/wiki/Western_Union_(alliance) "Western Union (alliance)") (WU), followed by the [International Authority for the Ruhr](/wiki/International_Authority_for_the_Ruhr "International Authority for the Ruhr"). Furthermore, the [Organisation for European Economic Co-operation](/wiki/Organisation_for_European_Economic_Co-operation "Organisation for European Economic Co-operation") (OEEC), the predecessor of the OECD, was also founded in 1948 to manage the [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan"), which led to the Soviets creating [Comecon](/wiki/Comecon "Comecon") in response. The ensuing [Hague Congress](/wiki/Hague_Congress_(1948) "Hague Congress (1948)") of May 1948 was a pivotal moment in European integration, as it led to the creation of the [European Movement International](/wiki/European_Movement_International "European Movement International"), the [College of Europe](/wiki/College_of_Europe "College of Europe"),[[28]](#cite_note-34) and most importantly to the foundation of the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") on 5 May 1949 (which is now [Europe Day](/wiki/Europe_Day "Europe Day")). The Council of Europe was one of the first institutions to bring the sovereign states of (then only Western) Europe together, raising great hopes and fevered debates in the following two years for further European integration.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It has since been a broad forum to further cooperation and shared issues, achieving for example the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights") in 1950.

Essential for the actual birth of the institutions of the EU was the [Schuman Declaration](/wiki/Schuman_Declaration "Schuman Declaration") on 9 May 1950 (the day after the fifth [Victory in Europe Day](/wiki/Victory_in_Europe_Day "Victory in Europe Day")) and the decision by six nations (France, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg, West Germany, and Italy) to follow Schuman and draft the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1951) "Treaty of Paris (1951)"). This treaty was created in 1952 the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community") (ECSC), which was built on the [International Authority for the Ruhr](/wiki/International_Authority_for_the_Ruhr "International Authority for the Ruhr"), installed by the Western Allies in 1949 to regulate the coal and steel industries of the Ruhr area in West Germany.[[29]](#cite_note-35) Backed by the [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") with large funds coming from the United States since 1948, the ECSC became a milestone organisation, enabling European economic development and integration and being the origin of the main institutions of the EU such as the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") and [Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament").[[30]](#cite_note-36)

[Founding fathers of the European Union](/wiki/Founding_fathers_of_the_European_Union "Founding fathers of the European Union") understood that coal and steel were the two industries essential for waging war, and believed that by tying their national industries together, a future war between their nations became much less likely.[[31]](#cite_note-Europa_History_45-59-37) In parallel with Schuman, the [Pleven Plan](/wiki/Pleven_Plan "Pleven Plan") of 1951 tried but failed to tie the institutions of the developing European community under the [European Political Community](/wiki/European_Political_Community_(1952) "European Political Community (1952)"), which was to include the also proposed [European Defence Community](/wiki/Treaty_establishing_the_European_Defence_Community "Treaty establishing the European Defence Community"), an alternative to [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") joining [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") which was established in 1949 under the [Truman Doctrine](/wiki/Truman_Doctrine "Truman Doctrine"). In 1954 the [Modified Brussels Treaty](/wiki/Modified_Brussels_Treaty "Modified Brussels Treaty") transformed the Western Union into the [Western European Union](/wiki/Western_European_Union "Western European Union") (WEU). [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") eventually joined both the WEU and NATO in 1955, prompting the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") to form the [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") in 1955 as an institutional framework for its military domination in the countries of [Central and Eastern Europe](/wiki/Central_and_Eastern_Europe "Central and Eastern Europe"). Assessing the progress of European integration the [Messina Conference](/wiki/Messina_Conference "Messina Conference") was held in 1955, ordering the [Spaak report](/wiki/Spaak_report "Spaak report"), which in 1956 recommended the next significant steps of European integration.

### Treaty of Rome (1958‍–‍1972)

Main article: [History of the European Communities (1958–1972)](/wiki/History_of_the_European_Communities_(1958%E2%80%931972) "History of the European Communities (1958–1972)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-45653-0001%2C_Rom%2C_Vertr%C3%A4ge_%C3%BCber_Zollpakt_und_Eurotom_unterzeichnet.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-45653-0001%2C_Rom%2C_Vertr%C3%A4ge_%C3%BCber_Zollpakt_und_Eurotom_unterzeichnet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-45653-0001,_Rom,_Vertr%C3%A4ge_%C3%BCber_Zollpakt_und_Eurotom_unterzeichnet.jpg)

Signing ceremony of the [Treaty of Rome](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rome "Treaty of Rome") (1957), establishing the [EEC](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community")

In 1957, [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), [the Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") signed the [Treaty of Rome](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rome "Treaty of Rome"), which created the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") (EEC) and established a [customs union](/wiki/European_Union_Customs_Union "European Union Customs Union").[[32]](#cite_note-38) They also signed another pact creating the [European Atomic Energy Community](/wiki/European_Atomic_Energy_Community "European Atomic Energy Community") (Euratom) for cooperation in developing nuclear power. Both treaties came into force in 1958.[[31]](#cite_note-Europa_History_45-59-37) Although the EEC and Euratom were created separately from the ECSC, they shared the same courts and the Common Assembly. The EEC was headed by [Walter Hallstein](/wiki/Walter_Hallstein "Walter Hallstein") ([Hallstein Commission](/wiki/Hallstein_Commission "Hallstein Commission")) and Euratom was headed by [Louis Armand](/wiki/Louis_Armand "Louis Armand") ([Armand Commission](/wiki/Armand_Commission "Armand Commission")) and then [Étienne Hirsch](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Hirsch "Étienne Hirsch") ([Hirsch Commission](/wiki/Hirsch_Commission "Hirsch Commission")).[[33]](#cite_note-39)[[34]](#cite_note-40)

The OEEC was in turn reformed in 1961 into the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development") (OECD) and its membership was extended to states outside of Europe, the United States and Canada. During the 1960s, tensions began to show, with France seeking to limit supranational power. Nevertheless, in 1965 an agreement was reached, and on 1 July 1967 the [Merger Treaty](/wiki/Merger_Treaty "Merger Treaty") created a single set of institutions for the three communities, which were collectively referred to as the *[European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities")*.[[35]](#cite_note-ENA_Merge-41) [Jean Rey](/wiki/Jean_Rey_(politician) "Jean Rey (politician)") [presided over](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") the first merged commission ([Rey Commission](/wiki/Rey_Commission "Rey Commission")).[[36]](#cite_note-42)

### First enlargement and European co-operation (1973‍–‍1993)

Main article: [History of the European Communities (1973–1993)](/wiki/History_of_the_European_Communities_(1973%E2%80%931993) "History of the European Communities (1973–1993)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Photograph_of_President_Gerald_R._Ford_and_Members_of_the_American_Delegation_Taking_a_Break_at_the_Conference_on_Security_and_Cooperation_in_Europe_in_Helsinki%2C_Finland_-_NARA_-_7462031.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photograph_of_President_Gerald_R._Ford_and_Members_of_the_American_Delegation_Taking_a_Break_at_the_Conference_on_Security_and_Cooperation_in_Europe_in_Helsinki,_Finland_-_NARA_-_7462031.jpg)

[Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") and the American delegation at the [CSCE](/wiki/Conference_on_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Conference on Security and Co-operation in Europe") (1975)

In 1973, the communities were enlarged to include [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") (including Greenland), Ireland, and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Accession_of_the_United_Kingdom_to_the_European_Communities "Accession of the United Kingdom to the European Communities").[[37]](#cite_note-ENA_First_enlargement-43) [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") had negotiated to join at the same time, but Norwegian voters rejected membership in a [referendum](/wiki/1972_Norwegian_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1972 Norwegian European Communities membership referendum"). The *[Ostpolitik](/wiki/Ostpolitik "Ostpolitik")* and the ensuing détente led to establishment of a first truly pan-European body, the [Conference on Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Conference_on_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Conference on Security and Co-operation in Europe") (CSCE), predecessor of the modern [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") (OSCE). In 1979, the [first direct elections](/wiki/1979_European_Parliament_election "1979 European Parliament election") to the European Parliament were held.[[38]](#cite_note-ENA_New_Parliament-44) [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") joined in 1981. In 1985, Greenland [left the Communities](/wiki/1982_Greenlandic_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1982 Greenlandic European Communities membership referendum"), following a dispute over fishing rights. During the same year, the [Schengen Agreement](/wiki/Schengen_Agreement "Schengen Agreement") paved the way for the creation of open borders without passport controls between most member states and some non-member states.[[39]](#cite_note-45) In 1986, the [Single European Act](/wiki/Single_European_Act "Single European Act") was signed. Portugal and [Spain](/wiki/Accession_Treaty_of_Spain_to_the_European_Economic_Community "Accession Treaty of Spain to the European Economic Community") joined in 1986.[[40]](#cite_note-ENA_Enlargement_negotiations-46) In 1990, after [the fall of the Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989"), the former [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") became part of the communities as part of a [reunified Germany](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification").[[41]](#cite_note-Europa_History_80-89-47)

### Treaties of Maastricht, Amsterdam and Nice (1993‍–‍2004)

Main article: [History of the European Union (1993–2004)](/wiki/History_of_the_European_Union_(1993%E2%80%932004) "History of the European Union (1993–2004)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/GER_%E2%80%94_BY_%E2%80%94_Regensburg_-_Donaumarkt_1_%28Museum_der_Bayerischen_Geschichte%3B_Vertrag_von_Maastricht%29_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-GER_%E2%80%94_BY_%E2%80%94_Regensburg_-_Donaumarkt_1_%28Museum_der_Bayerischen_Geschichte%3B_Vertrag_von_Maastricht%29_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:GER_%E2%80%94_BY_%E2%80%94_Regensburg_-_Donaumarkt_1_(Museum_der_Bayerischen_Geschichte;_Vertrag_von_Maastricht)_(cropped).JPG)

[Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty") (1992), establishing the EU

The European Union was formally established when the [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty")—whose main architects were [Horst Köhler](/wiki/Horst_K%C3%B6hler "Horst Köhler"),[[42]](#cite_note-48) [Helmut Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl") and [François Mitterrand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Mitterrand "François Mitterrand")—came into force on 1 November 1993.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraigDe_Burca201115-25)[[43]](#cite_note-49) The treaty also gave the name *[European Community](/wiki/European_Community "European Community")* to the EEC, even if it was referred to as such before the treaty. With further enlargement planned to include the former [communist states](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") of Central and Eastern Europe, as well as [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") and [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), the [Copenhagen criteria](/wiki/Copenhagen_criteria "Copenhagen criteria") for candidate members to join the EU were agreed upon in June 1993. The expansion of the EU introduced a new level of complexity and discord.[[44]](#cite_note-50)

In 1995, Austria, Finland, and Sweden [joined](/wiki/1995_enlargement_of_the_European_Union "1995 enlargement of the European Union") the EU. In 2002, euro banknotes and coins replaced national currencies in 12 of the member states. Since then, the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") has increased to encompass 21 countries. The euro currency became the second-largest reserve currency in the world. In 2004, the EU saw [its biggest enlargement to date](/wiki/2004_enlargement_of_the_European_Union "2004 enlargement of the European Union") when Cyprus, the Czech Republic, [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") joined the union.[[45]](#cite_note-Europa_History_00-51)

### Treaty of Lisbon and Brexit (2004‍–‍present)

Main article: [History of the European Union (2004–present)](/wiki/History_of_the_European_Union_(2004%E2%80%93present) "History of the European Union (2004–present)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Tratado_de_Lisboa_13_12_2007_%2804%29.jpg/250px-Tratado_de_Lisboa_13_12_2007_%2804%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tratado_de_Lisboa_13_12_2007_(04).jpg)

Signing ceremony of the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") (2007)

In 2007, Bulgaria and Romania became EU members. Later that year, Slovenia adopted the euro,[[45]](#cite_note-Europa_History_00-51) followed by Cyprus and Malta in 2008, Slovakia in 2009, Estonia in 2011, Latvia in 2014, Lithuania in 2015, and Croatia in 2023. On 1 December 2009, the [Lisbon Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") entered into force and reformed many aspects of the EU. In particular, it changed the legal structure of the EU, merging the [EU three pillars](/wiki/Three_pillars_of_the_European_Union "Three pillars of the European Union") system into a single legal entity provisioned with a [legal personality](/wiki/Legal_personality "Legal personality"), created a permanent [president of the European Council](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Council "President of the European Council"), the first of which was [Herman Van Rompuy](/wiki/Herman_Van_Rompuy "Herman Van Rompuy"), and strengthened the position of the [high representative of the union for foreign affairs and security policy](/wiki/High_Representative_of_the_Union_for_Foreign_Affairs_and_Security_Policy "High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiris2010448-52)[[47]](#cite_note-53)

In 2012, the EU [received the Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/2012_Nobel_Peace_Prize "2012 Nobel Peace Prize") for having "contributed to the advancement of peace and reconciliation, democracy, and human rights in Europe".[[48]](#cite_note-54)[[49]](#cite_note-55) In 2013, [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") became the 28th EU member.[[50]](#cite_note-56) From the beginning of the 2010s, the cohesion of the European Union has been tested by several issues, including the [Euro area crisis](/wiki/Euro_area_crisis "Euro area crisis"), [2015 European migrant crisis](/wiki/2015_European_migrant_crisis "2015 European migrant crisis"), and [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit").[[51]](#cite_note-BBCUKREFRESLT-57) A [referendum in the UK on its membership of the European Union](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") was held in 2016, with 51.9 per cent of participants voting to leave.[[52]](#cite_note-NYTEUBrexit-58) The UK formally notified the European Council of its decision to leave on 29 March 2017, initiating the [formal withdrawal procedure for leaving the EU](/wiki/Article_50_of_the_Treaty_on_European_Union "Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union"); following extensions to the process, the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") left the European Union on 31 January 2020, although most areas of EU law continued to apply to the UK for a transition period which lasted until 31 December 2020.[[53]](#cite_note-59)

The early 2020s saw Denmark [abolishing one of its three opt-outs](/wiki/2022_Danish_European_Union_opt-out_referendum "2022 Danish European Union opt-out referendum") and Croatia [adopting the euro](/wiki/Croatia_and_the_euro "Croatia and the euro"). After the [economic crisis](/wiki/COVID-19_recession "COVID-19 recession") caused by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the EU leaders agreed for the first time to create common debt to finance the European Recovery Program called [Next Generation EU](/wiki/Next_Generation_EU "Next Generation EU") (NGEU).[[54]](#cite_note-60) On 24 February 2022, after massing on the borders of Ukraine, the [Russian Armed Forces](/wiki/Russian_Armed_Forces "Russian Armed Forces") undertook an attempt for a full-scale invasion of Ukraine.[[55]](#cite_note-61)[[56]](#cite_note-62) The European Union imposed [heavy sanctions on Russia](/wiki/International_sanctions_during_the_Russo-Ukrainian_War#EU_sanctions "International sanctions during the Russo-Ukrainian War") and agreed on a pooled military aid package to Ukraine for lethal weapons funded via the [European Peace Facility](/wiki/European_Peace_Facility "European Peace Facility") off-budget instrument.[[57]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/The_ancient_Roman_Agora_is_illuminated_during_the_Next_Gen_EU_event%2C_in_Athens%2C_Greece%2C_on_June_16%2C_2021.jpg/330px-The_ancient_Roman_Agora_is_illuminated_during_the_Next_Gen_EU_event%2C_in_Athens%2C_Greece%2C_on_June_16%2C_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_ancient_Roman_Agora_is_illuminated_during_the_Next_Gen_EU_event,_in_Athens,_Greece,_on_June_16,_2021.jpg)

The ancient [Roman Agora](/wiki/Roman_Agora "Roman Agora") in Athens illuminated with a Next Generation EU sign

NGEU is a European Commission [economic recovery](/wiki/Economic_recovery "Economic recovery") package to support the EU member states to recover from the COVID-19 pandemic, in particular those that have been particularly hard hit. It is sometimes styled NextGenerationEU and Next Gen EU, and also called the European Union Recovery Instrument.[[58]](#cite_note-64) Agreed in principle by the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") on 21 July 2020 and adopted on 14 December 2020, the instrument is worth [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")750 billion. NGEU will operate from 2021 to 2026,[[59]](#cite_note-65) and will be tied to the regular [2021–2027 budget of the EU's Multiannual Financial Framework](/wiki/Multiannual_Financial_Framework#2021–2027_Financial_Framework_and_recovery_package "Multiannual Financial Framework") (MFF). The comprehensive NGEU and MFF packages are projected to reach €1824.3 billion.[[60]](#cite_note-66)

Preparing the EU for a new great enlargement is one of its political priorities, with the goal of achieving 35 member states by 2030. Institutional and budgetary reforms are being discussed in order for the EU to be ready for the new members.[[61]](#cite_note-67)[[62]](#cite_note-68)[[63]](#cite_note-69)[[64]](#cite_note-70)

Ahead of the elections in June 2024, the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)") led to inflation, lowering life level and created a possibility of strong changes in the elections.[[65]](#cite_note-71)[[66]](#cite_note-72)

In December 2025, it was leaked that the [United States government](/wiki/United_States_government "United States government") would attempt to persuade "Austria, Hungary, Italy, and Poland" to depart the EU in a Brexit-type manner.[[67]](#cite_note-DO_Trump_interfere_2025-12-09-73)

### Timeline

Since the [end of World War II](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_II "Aftermath of World War II"), most [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") [European countries](/wiki/European_countries "European countries") have entered into treaties and thereby co-operated and harmonised policies (or *pooled sovereignty*) in an increasing number of areas, in the [European integration project](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") or the *construction of Europe* ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *la construction européenne*). The following timeline outlines the legal inception of the European Union (EU)—the principal framework for this unification. The EU inherited many of its present [organisations](/wiki/Bodies_of_the_European_Union_and_Euratom "Bodies of the European Union and Euratom"), [institutions](/wiki/Institutions_of_the_European_Union "Institutions of the European Union"), and responsibilities from the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") (EC), which were founded in the 1950s in the spirit of the [Schuman Declaration](/wiki/Schuman_Declaration "Schuman Declaration").

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-ref-1_74-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-ref-1_74-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-ref-1_74-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-ref-1_74-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-ref-1_74-4) Although not EU treaties *per se*, these treaties affected the [development](/wiki/History_of_the_Common_Security_and_Defence_Policy "History of the Common Security and Defence Policy") of the EU defence arm, a main part of the CFSP. The Franco-British alliance established by the Dunkirk Treaty was *de facto* superseded by WU. The CFSP pillar was bolstered by some of the security structures that had been established within the remit of the 1955 [Modified Brussels Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brussels "Treaty of Brussels") (MBT). The Brussels Treaty was [terminated](/wiki/Western_European_Union#2009–2011:_Dissolution "Western European Union") in 2011, consequently dissolving the WEU, as the [mutual defence clause](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Consolidated_version_of_the_Treaty_on_European_Union/Title_V:_General_Provisions_on_the_Union%27s_External_Action_Service_and_Specific_Provisions_on_the_Common_Foreign_and_Security_Policy#Article_42 "wikisource:Consolidated version of the Treaty on European Union/Title V: General Provisions on the Union's External Action Service and Specific Provisions on the Common Foreign and Security Policy") that the Lisbon Treaty provided for EU was considered to render the WEU superfluous. The EU thus *de facto* superseded the WEU.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-ref-6_75-0)** Plans to establish a [European Political Community](/wiki/Treaty_establishing_the_European_Political_Community "Treaty establishing the European Political Community") (EPC) were shelved following the French failure to ratify the [Treaty establishing the European Defence Community](/wiki/Treaty_establishing_the_European_Defence_Community "Treaty establishing the European Defence Community") (EDC). The EPC would have combined the ECSC and the EDC.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-ref-3_76-0)** The [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") obtained common institutions and a shared [legal personality](/wiki/Legal_person "Legal person") (i.e. ability to e.g. sign treaties in their own right).
4. **[^](#cite_ref-ref-2_77-0)** The treaties of Maastricht and Rome form the EU's [legal basis](/wiki/Treaties_of_the_European_Union "Treaties of the European Union"), and are also referred to as the [Treaty on European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_European_Union "Treaty on European Union") (TEU) and the [Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Functioning_of_the_European_Union "Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union") (TFEU), respectively. They are amended by secondary treaties.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-ref-4_78-0)** Between the EU's founding in 1993 and consolidation in 2009, the union consisted of [three pillars](/wiki/Three_pillars_of_the_European_Union "Three pillars of the European Union"), the first of which were the European Communities. The other two pillars consisted of additional areas of cooperation that had been added to the EU's remit.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-ref-5_79-0)** The consolidation meant that the EU inherited the European Communities' [legal personality](/wiki/Legal_person "Legal person") and that the [pillar system was abolished](/wiki/Three_pillars_of_the_European_Union "Three pillars of the European Union"), resulting in the EU framework as such covering all policy areas. Executive/legislative power in each area was instead determined by a [distribution of competencies](/wiki/Template:EU_competences "Template:EU competences") between [EU institutions](/wiki/Institutions_of_the_European_Union "Institutions of the European Union") and [member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union"). This distribution, as well as treaty provisions for policy areas in which unanimity is required and [qualified majority voting](/wiki/Double_majority#European_Union "Double majority") is possible, reflects the depth of EU integration as well as the EU's partly [supranational](/wiki/Supranational_union "Supranational union") and partly [intergovernmental](/wiki/Intergovernmentalism "Intergovernmentalism") nature.

## Politics

Main article: [Politics of the European Union](/wiki/Politics_of_the_European_Union "Politics of the European Union")

The European Union operates through a hybrid system of [supranational](/wiki/Supranational_union "Supranational union") and [intergovernmental](/wiki/Intergovernmentalism "Intergovernmentalism") decision-making,[[68]](#cite_note-Britannica-80)[[69]](#cite_note-CIA-81) and according to the [principle of conferral](/wiki/Principle_of_conferral "Principle of conferral") (which says that it should act only within the limits of the competences conferred on it by the [treaties](/wiki/Treaties_of_the_European_Union "Treaties of the European Union")) and of [subsidiarity](/wiki/Subsidiarity_(European_Union) "Subsidiarity (European Union)") (which says that it should act only where an objective cannot be sufficiently achieved by the member states acting alone). [Laws made by the EU institutions](/wiki/European_Union_law "European Union law") are passed in a variety of forms.[[70]](#cite_note-82) Generally speaking, they can be classified into two groups: those which come into force without the necessity for national implementation measures (regulations) and those which specifically require national implementation measures (directives).[[g]](#cite_note-83) EU policy is in general promulgated by [EU directives](/wiki/Directive_(European_Union) "Directive (European Union)"), which are then implemented in the [domestic legislation](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") of its [member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union"), and [EU regulations](/wiki/Regulation_(European_Union) "Regulation (European Union)"), which are immediately enforceable in all member states. [Lobbying](/wiki/Lobbying#European_Union "Lobbying") at the EU level by special interest groups is regulated to try to balance the aspirations of private initiatives with public interest decision-making process.[[71]](#cite_note-84)

### Member states

Main article: [Member state of the European Union](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Member_States_of_the_European_Union_%28polar_stereographic_projection%29_EN.svg/500px-Member_States_of_the_European_Union_%28polar_stereographic_projection%29_EN.svg.png)

Map showing the member states of the European Union (clickable)

Through successive [enlargements](/wiki/Enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Enlargement of the European Union"), the EU and its predecessors have grown from the [six founding states](/wiki/Inner_Six "Inner Six") of the EEC to 27 members. Countries accede to the union by becoming a party to the founding [treaties](/wiki/Treaties_of_the_European_Union "Treaties of the European Union"), thereby subjecting themselves to the privileges and obligations of EU membership. This entails a partial delegation of sovereignty to the institutions in return for representation within those institutions, a practice often referred to as "pooling of sovereignty".[[72]](#cite_note-85)[[73]](#cite_note-86) In some policies, there are several member states that ally with strategic partners within the union. Examples of such alliances include the [Baltic Assembly](/wiki/Baltic_Assembly "Baltic Assembly"), the [Benelux Union](/wiki/Benelux_Union "Benelux Union"), the [Bucharest Nine](/wiki/Bucharest_Nine "Bucharest Nine"), the [Craiova Group](/wiki/Craiova_Group "Craiova Group"), the [EU Med Group](/wiki/EU_Med_Group "EU Med Group"), the [Lublin Triangle](/wiki/Lublin_Triangle "Lublin Triangle"), the [New Hanseatic League](/wiki/New_Hanseatic_League "New Hanseatic League"), the [Three Seas Initiative](/wiki/Three_Seas_Initiative "Three Seas Initiative"), the [Visegrád Group](/wiki/Visegr%C3%A1d_Group "Visegrád Group"), and the [Weimar Triangle](/wiki/Weimar_Triangle "Weimar Triangle").

To become a member, a country must meet the [Copenhagen criteria](/wiki/Copenhagen_criteria "Copenhagen criteria"), defined at the 1993 meeting of the European Council in Copenhagen. These require a stable democracy that respects human rights and the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law"); a functioning [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy"); and the acceptance of the obligations of membership, including EU law. Evaluation of a country's fulfilment of the criteria is the responsibility of the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council").[[74]](#cite_note-Accession_Criteria-87)

The four countries forming the [European Free Trade Association](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association") (EFTA) are not EU members, but have partly committed to the EU's economy and regulations: Iceland, [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein") and Norway, which are a part of the [single market](/wiki/Single_market "Single market") through the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area"), and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), which has similar ties through [bilateral treaties](/wiki/Switzerland%E2%80%93European_Union_relations "Switzerland–European Union relations").[[75]](#cite_note-EEA-88)[[76]](#cite_note-CH-89) The relationships of the [European microstates](/wiki/Microstates_and_the_European_Union "Microstates and the European Union") [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra"), [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino"), and [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") include the use of the euro and other areas of co-operation.[[77]](#cite_note-euro_use_world-90)

#### Subdivisions

Main article: [Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics](/wiki/Nomenclature_of_Territorial_Units_for_Statistics "Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics")

[Subdivisions of member-states](/wiki/Country_subdivision "Country subdivision") are based on the Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics (NUTS), a [geocode](/wiki/Geocode "Geocode") standard for statistical purposes. The [standard](/wiki/Standardization "Standardization"), adopted in 2003, is developed and regulated by the European Union, and thus only covers the [member states of the EU](/wiki/Member_State_of_the_European_Union "Member State of the European Union") in detail. The Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics is instrumental in the European Union's [Structural Funds and Cohesion Fund](/wiki/Structural_Funds_and_Cohesion_Fund "Structural Funds and Cohesion Fund") delivery mechanisms and for locating the area where goods and services subject to European [public procurement](/wiki/Government_procurement_in_the_European_Union "Government procurement in the European Union") legislation are to be delivered.

#### Former members

[Article 50](/wiki/Article_50_of_the_Treaty_on_European_Union "Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union") of the [Lisbon Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") provides the basis for a member to [leave the EU](/wiki/Withdrawal_from_the_European_Union "Withdrawal from the European Union"). Two territories have left the union: [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") (an [autonomous province](/wiki/Autonomous_area "Autonomous area") of Denmark) withdrew in 1985;[[86]](#cite_note-100) the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") formally invoked Article 50 of the Consolidated Treaty on European Union in 2017, and became the only sovereign state to leave when it [withdrew](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") from the EU in 2020.

### Governance

Main articles: [Bodies of the European Union and the Euratom](/wiki/Bodies_of_the_European_Union_and_the_Euratom "Bodies of the European Union and the Euratom"), [Institutions of the European Union](/wiki/Institutions_of_the_European_Union "Institutions of the European Union"), and [Subsidiarity (European Union) § EU competences](/wiki/Subsidiarity_(European_Union)#EU_competences "Subsidiarity (European Union)")

Member states retain in principle all powers except those that they have agreed collectively to delegate to the union as a whole, though the exact delimitation has on occasions become a subject of scholarly or legal disputes.[[87]](#cite_note-101)[[88]](#cite_note-102) In certain fields, members have awarded [exclusive competence and exclusive mandate](/wiki/Subsidiarity_(European_Union)#EU_competences "Subsidiarity (European Union)") to the union. These are areas in which member states have entirely renounced their own capacity to enact legislation. In other areas, the EU and its member states share the competence to legislate. While both can legislate, the member states can only legislate to the extent to which the EU has not. In other policy areas, the EU can only co-ordinate, support and supplement member state action but cannot enact legislation with the aim of harmonising national laws.[[89]](#cite_note-103) That a particular policy area falls into a certain category of competence is not necessarily indicative of what [legislative procedure](/wiki/Legislature_of_the_European_Union "Legislature of the European Union") is used for enacting legislation within that policy area. Different legislative procedures are used within the same category of competence, and even with the same policy area. The distribution of competences in various policy areas between member states and the union is divided into the following three categories:

**[Competences](/wiki/Competences_of_the_European_Union "Competences of the European Union") of the European Union in relation to those of its [member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union")**[[90]](#cite_note-104)

The European Union has seven principal decision-making bodies, its [institutions](/wiki/Institutions_of_the_European_Union "Institutions of the European Union"): the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"), the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council"), the [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union"), the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), the [Court of Justice of the European Union](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union"), the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") and the [European Court of Auditors](/wiki/European_Court_of_Auditors "European Court of Auditors"). Competence in scrutinising and amending legislation is shared between the Council of the European Union and the European Parliament, while executive tasks are performed by the European Commission and in a limited capacity by the European Council (not to be confused with the aforementioned Council of the European Union). The [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") of the eurozone is determined by the European Central Bank. The interpretation and the application of EU law and the treaties are ensured by the Court of Justice of the European Union. The EU budget is scrutinised by the European Court of Auditors. There are also a number of ancillary bodies which advise the EU or operate in a specific area.

### Branches of power

Main article: [Bodies of the European Union and the Euratom](/wiki/Bodies_of_the_European_Union_and_the_Euratom "Bodies of the European Union and the Euratom")

#### Executive branch

Main articles: [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") and [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission")

[![Portrait of António Costa](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Antonio_Costa_Portrait_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Antonio_Costa_Portrait_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antonio_Costa_Portrait_2024_(cropped).jpg)

[António Costa](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Costa "António Costa"),  
[President of the European Council](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Council "President of the European Council")

[![Portrait of Ursula von der Leyen](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Official_Portrait_of_Ursula_von_der_Leyen_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Official_Portrait_of_Ursula_von_der_Leyen_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_Portrait_of_Ursula_von_der_Leyen_(cropped).jpg)

[Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen"),  
[President of the European Commission](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission")

The union's executive branch is organised as a [directorial system](/wiki/Directorial_system "Directorial system"), where the executive power is jointly exercised by several people. The executive branch consists of the European Council and European Commission.

The European Council sets the broad political direction of the union. It convenes at least four times a year and comprises the [president of the European Council](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Council "President of the European Council") (presently [António Costa](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Costa "António Costa")), the [president of the European Commission](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") and one representative per [member state](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") (either its [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") or [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government")). The [high representative of the union for foreign affairs and security policy](/wiki/High_Representative_of_the_Union_for_Foreign_Affairs_and_Security_Policy "High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy") (presently [Kaja Kallas](/wiki/Kaja_Kallas "Kaja Kallas")) also takes part in its meetings. Described by some as the union's "supreme political leadership",[[91]](#cite_note-How_work-105) it is actively involved in the negotiation of [treaty changes](/wiki/Treaties_of_the_European_Union "Treaties of the European Union") and defines the EU's policy agenda and strategies. Its leadership role involves solving disputes between member states and the institutions, and to resolving any political crises or disagreements over controversial issues and policies. It acts as a "[collective head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state")" and [ratifies](/wiki/Ratification "Ratification") important documents (for example, international agreements and treaties).[[92]](#cite_note-Oxford_University_Press-106) Tasks for the president of the European Council are ensuring the external representation of the EU,[[93]](#cite_note-Council_Press_Release-107) driving consensus and resolving divergences among member states, both during meetings of the European Council and over the periods between them. The European Council should not be mistaken for the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), an international organisation independent of the EU and based in Strasbourg.

The [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") acts both as the EU's [executive arm](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)"), responsible for the day-to-day running of the EU, and also the [legislative initiator](/wiki/Right_of_initiative_(legislative) "Right of initiative (legislative)"), with the sole power to propose laws for debate.[[94]](#cite_note-108)[[95]](#cite_note-Library_of_European_Parliament-109)[[96]](#cite_note-110) The commission is 'guardian of the Treaties' and is responsible for their efficient operation and policing.[[97]](#cite_note-111) It has 27 [European commissioners](/wiki/European_Commissioner "European Commissioner") for different areas of policy, one from each member state, though commissioners are bound to represent the interests of the EU as a whole rather than their home state. The leader of the 27 is the [president of the European Commission](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") (presently [Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen") for 2019–2024, reelected for the 2024–2029 term), [proposed by the European Council](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission#Appointment "President of the European Commission"), following and taking into account the result of the European elections, and is then elected by the European Parliament.[[98]](#cite_note-112) The President retains, as the leader responsible for the entire cabinet, the final say in accepting or rejecting a candidate submitted for a given portfolio by a member state, and oversees the commission's permanent civil service. After the President, the most prominent commissioner is the high representative of the union for foreign affairs and security policy, who is *ex-officio* a [vice-president of the European Commission](/wiki/Vice-President_of_the_European_Commission "Vice-President of the European Commission") and is also chosen by the European Council.[[99]](#cite_note-113) The other 25 commissioners are subsequently appointed by the [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union") in agreement with the nominated president. The 27 commissioners as a single body are subject to approval (or otherwise) by a vote of the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"). All commissioners are first nominated by the government of the respective member state.[[100]](#cite_note-114)

#### Legislative branch

Main articles: [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union") and [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament")

[![Portrait of Roberta Metsola](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Official_portrait_of_Roberta_Metsola_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Official_portrait_of_Roberta_Metsola_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_portrait_of_Roberta_Metsola_(cropped).jpg)

[Roberta Metsola](/wiki/Roberta_Metsola "Roberta Metsola"),  
[President of the European Parliament](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Parliament "President of the European Parliament")

The council, as it is now simply called[[101]](#cite_note-115) (also called the Council of the European Union[[102]](#cite_note-116) and the "Council of Ministers", its former title),[[103]](#cite_note-117) forms one half of the EU's legislature. It consists of a representative from each member state's government and meets in [different compositions depending on the policy area being addressed](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union#Configurations "Council of the European Union"). Notwithstanding its different configurations, it is considered to be one single body. In addition to the legislative functions, members of the council also have [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") responsibilities, such as the development of a [Common Foreign and Security Policy](/wiki/Common_Foreign_and_Security_Policy "Common Foreign and Security Policy") and the coordination of broad economic policies within the union.[[104]](#cite_note-Europa_Institutions_Council-118) The [Presidency of the council](/wiki/Presidency_of_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Presidency of the Council of the European Union") rotates between member states, with each holding it for six months. Beginning on 1 July 2025, the position is held by Denmark.[[2]](#cite_note-presidency-council-5)

The European Parliament is one of three [legislative institutions](/wiki/Legislature_of_the_European_Union "Legislature of the European Union") of the EU, which together with the [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union") is tasked with amending and approving the European Commission's proposals. 705 [members of the European Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") (MEPs) are directly [elected](/wiki/Elections_in_the_European_Union "Elections in the European Union") by [EU citizens](/wiki/Citizenship_of_the_European_Union "Citizenship of the European Union") every five years on the basis of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"). MEPs are elected on a national basis and they sit according to [political groups](/wiki/Political_groups_of_the_European_Parliament "Political groups of the European Parliament") rather than their nationality. Each country has a set number of seats and is divided into [sub-national constituencies](/wiki/European_Parliament_constituency "European Parliament constituency") where this does not affect the proportional nature of the voting system.[[105]](#cite_note-119) In the [ordinary legislative procedure](/wiki/Ordinary_legislative_procedure "Ordinary legislative procedure"), the European Commission proposes legislation, which requires the joint approval of the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union to pass. This process applies to nearly all areas, including the [EU budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union"). The parliament is the final body to approve or reject the proposed membership of the commission, and can attempt motions of censure on the commission by appeal to the [Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice"). The [president of the European Parliament](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Parliament "President of the European Parliament") carries out the role of speaker in Parliament and represents it externally. The president and [vice-presidents](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_European_Parliament "Vice President of the European Parliament") are elected by MEPs every two and a half years.[[106]](#cite_note-Europa_Institutions_Parliament-120)

#### Judicial branch

Main article: [Court of Justice of the European Union](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union")

[![Portrait of Koen Lenaerts](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Koen_Lenaerts_2022.jpg/250px-Koen_Lenaerts_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Koen_Lenaerts_2022.jpg)

[Koen Lenaerts](/wiki/Koen_Lenaerts "Koen Lenaerts"),  
[President of the Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice#President "European Court of Justice")

The [judicial branch](/wiki/Judicial_branch "Judicial branch") of the European Union is formally called the [Court of Justice of the European Union](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union") (CJEU) and consists of two courts: the [Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice") and the [General Court](/wiki/General_Court_(European_Union) "General Court (European Union)").[[107]](#cite_note-121) The [Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice") is the [supreme court](/wiki/Supreme_court "Supreme court") of the European Union in matters of [European Union law](/wiki/European_Union_law "European Union law"). As a part of the CJEU, it is tasked with interpreting EU law and ensuring its uniform application across all [EU member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") under Article 263 of the Treaty of the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU). The Court was established in 1952, and is based in [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City"). It is composed of one judge per [member state](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") – currently 27 – although it normally hears cases in panels of three, five or fifteen judges. The Court has been led by president [Koen Lenaerts](/wiki/Koen_Lenaerts "Koen Lenaerts") since 2015. The CJEU is the highest court of the European Union in matters of [Union law](/wiki/European_Union_law "European Union law"). Its case-law provides that EU law has supremacy over any national law that is inconsistent with EU law.[[108]](#cite_note-122) It is not possible to appeal against the decisions of national courts in the CJEU, but rather national courts refer questions of EU law to the CJEU (a preliminary reference). However, it is ultimately for the national court to apply the resulting interpretation (the preliminary ruling) to the facts of any given case. Although, only courts of final appeal are bound to refer a question of EU law when one is addressed. The treaties give the CJEU the power for consistent application of EU law across the EU as a whole. The court also acts as an administrative and constitutional court between the other EU institutions and the Member States and can annul or invalidate unlawful acts of EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies.

The [General Court](/wiki/General_Court_(European_Union) "General Court (European Union)") is a constituent court of the European Union. It hears actions taken against the [institutions of the European Union](/wiki/Institutions_of_the_European_Union "Institutions of the European Union") by individuals and member states, although certain matters are reserved for the Court of Justice. Decisions of the General Court can be appealed to the Court of Justice, but only on a point of law. Prior to the coming into force of the [Lisbon Treaty](/wiki/Lisbon_Treaty "Lisbon Treaty") on 1 December 2009, it was known as the Court of First Instance. Since the amendments to the [Statute of the CJEU](/wiki/Statute_of_the_Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Statute of the Court of Justice of the European Union") in October 2024, the Court of Justice shares its jurisdiction over references for a preliminary ruling with the General Court.[[109]](#cite_note-123) The General Court is since then competent to handle preliminary references falling under one of the six following areas: the common system of [value added tax](/wiki/European_Union_value_added_tax "European Union value added tax"); excise duties; the Customs Code; the tariff classification of goods under the [Combined Nomenclature](/wiki/Combined_Nomenclature "Combined Nomenclature"); compensation and assistance to passengers in the event of denied boarding or delay or cancellation of transport services; the scheme for greenhouse gas emission allowance trading.[[110]](#cite_note-124)
