[![Portrait of Christine Lagarde](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/EU2024BE_240223_Informal_meeting_of_economic_and_financial_affairs_ministers_Gent_JNZT_0197_%2853548338059%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-EU2024BE_240223_Informal_meeting_of_economic_and_financial_affairs_ministers_Gent_JNZT_0197_%2853548338059%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:EU2024BE_240223_Informal_meeting_of_economic_and_financial_affairs_ministers_Gent_JNZT_0197_(53548338059)_(cropped).jpg)

[Christine Lagarde](/wiki/Christine_Lagarde "Christine Lagarde"),  
[President of the European Central Bank](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Central_Bank "President of the European Central Bank")

The [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") (ECB) is one of the institutions of the [monetary branch](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") of the European Union, the prime component of the [Eurosystem](/wiki/Eurosystem "Eurosystem") and the European System of Central Banks. It is one of the world's [most important central banks](/wiki/Big_Four_(banking)#International_use "Big Four (banking)"). The [ECB Governing Council](/wiki/Governing_Council_of_the_European_Central_Bank "Governing Council of the European Central Bank") makes [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") for the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") and the European Union, administers the [foreign exchange reserves](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_reserves "Foreign exchange reserves") of EU member states, engages in foreign exchange operations, and defines the intermediate monetary objectives and key interest rate of the EU. The [ECB Executive Board](/wiki/Executive_Board_of_the_European_Central_Bank "Executive Board of the European Central Bank") enforces the policies and decisions of the Governing Council, and may direct the national central banks when doing so. The ECB has the exclusive right to authorise the issuance of [euro banknotes](/wiki/Euro_banknotes "Euro banknotes"). Member states can issue [euro coins](/wiki/Euro_coins "Euro coins"), but the volume must be approved by the ECB beforehand. The bank also operates the [T2 (RTGS)](/wiki/T2_(RTGS) "T2 (RTGS)") payments system. The [European System of Central Banks](/wiki/European_System_of_Central_Banks "European System of Central Banks") (ESCB) consists of the ECB and the national central banks (NCBs) of all 27 member states of the European Union. The ESCB is not the monetary authority of the eurozone, because not all EU member states have joined the euro. The ESCB's objective is price stability throughout the European Union. Secondarily, the ESCB's goal is to improve monetary and financial cooperation between the Eurosystem and member states outside the eurozone.

The [European Court of Auditors](/wiki/European_Court_of_Auditors "European Court of Auditors") (ECA) is the [auditory branch](/wiki/Supreme_audit_institution "Supreme audit institution") of the European Union. It was established in 1975 in [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City") in order to improve EU financial management. It has 27 members (1 from each EU member-state) supported by approximately 800 civil servants. The [European Personnel Selection Office](/wiki/European_Personnel_Selection_Office "European Personnel Selection Office") (EPSO) is the EU's [civil service recruitment body](/wiki/Civil_service_commission "Civil service commission") and operates its selection of candidates via generalist and specialist competitions. Each institution is then able to recruit staff from among the pool of candidates selected by EPSO. On average, EPSO receives around 60,000–70,000 applications a year with around 1,500–2,000 candidates recruited by the European Union institutions. The [European Ombudsman](/wiki/European_Ombudsman "European Ombudsman") is the [ombudsman branch](/wiki/Ombudsman "Ombudsman") of the European Union that holds the institutions, bodies and agencies of the EU to account, and promotes good administration. The Ombudsman helps people, businesses and organisations facing problems with the EU administration by investigating complaints, as well as by proactively looking into broader systemic issues. The current Ombudsman is [Teresa Anjinho](/wiki/Teresa_Anjinho "Teresa Anjinho")[[111]](#cite_note-125). The [European Public Prosecutor's Office](/wiki/European_Public_Prosecutor%27s_Office "European Public Prosecutor's Office") (EPPO) is the [prosecutory branch](/wiki/Attorney_general "Attorney general") of the union with juridical personality, established under the Treaty of Lisbon between 23 of the 27 states of the EU following the method of enhanced cooperation. It is based in [Kirchberg](/wiki/Kirchberg,_Luxembourg "Kirchberg, Luxembourg"), Luxembourg City alongside the Court of Justice of the European Union and the European Court of Auditors

### EU ethical governance and [Ethics Body](/wiki/European_Union_Ethics_Body "European Union Ethics Body")

The European Union’s public ethics system is highly fragmented, with various bespoke public integrity regimes coexisting under EU law, each possessing its own ethics principles and obligations ([Alberto Alemanno](/wiki/Alberto_Alemanno "Alberto Alemanno"), *The Law and Governance of the EU Public Ethics System*[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126), Chapter 2, p. 15). EU ethics standards are scattered across multiple legal sources, ranging from the EU Treaties and institutional Rules of Procedure to Codes of Conduct and the EU Staff Regulations (Alberto Alemanno, Chapter 2, p. 16[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)). While public ethics instruments exist in almost all EU institutions, there is less evidence that ethics constitutes an integrated feature of day-to-day EU policymaking, a concept defined by scholars as "ethical governance" (Michelle Cini and Andreea Năstase, Chapter 3, p. 49[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)).

Historically, the system's enforcement mechanisms have faced severe structural limitations, notably high fragmentation, limited independence, and weak sanctioning powers (Alberto Alemanno, Chapter 2, p. 26[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)). Furthermore, virtually all EU institutions have traditionally entrusted the respect of these rules to themselves through internal self-assessment mechanisms rather than independent external oversight (Alberto Alemanno, Chapter 13, p. 278[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)). The 2022 [Qatargate](/wiki/Qatar_corruption_scandal_at_the_European_Parliament "Qatar corruption scandal at the European Parliament") influence-buying scandal served as a critical juncture that accelerated the push for systemic reform across the institutions (Dionyssis G. Dimitrakopoulos, Chapter 9, p. 186[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)), as public perception of the transparency and independence of EU institutions vis-à-vis private interests remained poor (Silvia Kotanidis and Titouan Faucheux, *EPRS Briefing*, p. 2[[113]](#cite_note-:2-127)). In 2021, the European Parliament had initially proposed the creation of a strong, independent ethics body equipped with its own investigative and enforcement powers (Silvia Kotanidis and Titouan Faucheux, *EPRS Briefing*, pp. 4-5[[113]](#cite_note-:2-127)).

Instead, the main EU institutions concluded an agreement, formally signed in May 2024, to establish a comparatively constrained Interinstitutional Body for Ethical Standards (IBES) (Alberto Alemanno, Chapter 13, p. 279; Silvia Kotanidis and Titouan Faucheux, *EPRS Briefing*, p. 8[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)). The core task of this new entity is the development of "common minimum standards" for the conduct of the political members of the participating institutions, strictly excluding the [EU civil service staff](/wiki/European_Civil_Service "European Civil Service") (Alberto Alemanno, Chapter 13, p. 283; Zachariah Davies and Arnaud Van Waeyenberge, Chapter 12, p. 272[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)). The new body adopts these minimum standards by consensus and is assisted by five independent experts (Silvia Kotanidis and Titouan Faucheux, *EPRS Briefing*, p. 9[[113]](#cite_note-:2-127)). [The Court of Justice of the EU](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union") (ECJ) participates only as an observer to preserve its judicial impartiality (Silvia Kotanidis and Titouan Faucheux, *EPRS Briefing*, p. 8[[113]](#cite_note-:2-127)).

The [Interinstitutional EU Ethics Body](/wiki/European_Union_Ethics_Body "European Union Ethics Body") (or IBES, for Interinstitutional Body for Ethical Standards, as mentioned in the EPRS briefing[[113]](#cite_note-:2-127)) faces specific legal and constitutional constraints. Functioning through the "contractual" conjunction of each participating institution, the body operates primarily as a formal mechanism for coordination rather than a centralized enforcement agency (Julio Baquero Cruz, Chapter 5, pp. 88, 94-95[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)). Curiously the body is not based on the Treaty legal basis for IIAs ([Inter Institutional Agreements](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/interinstitutional-agreements.html)) (Article 295 TFEU[[114]](#cite_note-128)), as this article only refers to the three main EU institutions. It cannot take over decision-making powers for individual disciplinary cases (Julio Baquero Cruz, Chapter 5, p. 98; Silvia Kotanidis and Titouan Faucheux, *EPRS Briefing*, p. 6[[113]](#cite_note-:2-127)). Consequently, it does not replace existing ethical authorities but merely aims at complementing them, and its creation explicitly does not encroach upon the investigative competences of the European Anti-Fraud Office ([OLAF](/wiki/European_Anti-Fraud_Office "European Anti-Fraud Office")) or the [European Ombudsman](/wiki/European_Ombudsman "European Ombudsman") (Silvia Kotanidis and Titouan Faucheux, *EPRS Briefing*, p. 10[[113]](#cite_note-:2-127); M. Martínez Navarro, *Le Médiateur européen*, p. 239[[115]](#cite_note-:3-129)). The [European Ombudsman](/wiki/European_Ombudsman "European Ombudsman") continues to play a parallel role in assessing ethical breaches and conflicts of interests under the broader concept of "maladministration," which goes beyond strict illegality (M. Martínez Navarro, *Le Médiateur européen*, pp. 94, 237[[115]](#cite_note-:3-129)).

Academic evaluations and civil society groups generally characterize the creation of the IBES as an example of "incremental change" rather than comprehensive reform (Dionyssis G. Dimitrakopoulos, Chapter 9, pp. 192-193[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)). Civil society organizations, such as [Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International")[[116]](#cite_note-130), have criticized the final agreement as "toothless" due to its inability to independently investigate or sanction the EU institutions (Silvia Kotanidis and Titouan Faucheux, *EPRS Briefing*, p. 11[[113]](#cite_note-:2-127)). Similarly, legal scholars point out that the body lacks investigative powers and the capacity to apply ethical standards to concrete individual cases (M. Martínez Navarro, *Le Médiateur européen*, p. 239[[115]](#cite_note-:3-129)). Nevertheless, because the body requires each participating institution to conduct a public, written self-assessment of its internal rules, experts suggest that the ensuing public scrutiny and institutional peer pressure may induce institutions to align their standards more closely over time (Alberto Alemanno, Chapter 13, p. 284; Dionyssis G. Dimitrakopoulos, Chapter 9, p. 193[[112]](#cite_note-:1-126)). See the article [EU Ethics Body](/wiki/European_Union_Ethics_Body "European Union Ethics Body") to learn more.

### Budget

Main article: [Budget of the European Union](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union")

**EU funding programmes 2014–2020  
(€1,087 billion)**[[117]](#cite_note-131)

1. Sustainable Growth/Natural Resources (38.6%)
2. Competitiveness for Growth and Jobs (13.1%)
3. Global Europe (6.10%)
4. Economic, Territorial and Social Cohesion (34.1%)
5. Administration (6.40%)
6. Security and Citizenship (1.70%)

The European Union had an agreed budget of €170.6 billion in 2022. The EU had a long-term budget of €1,082.5 billion for the period 2014–2020, representing 1.02% of the EU-28's GNI. In 1960, the budget of the [European Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") was 0.03 per cent of GDP.[[118]](#cite_note-132) Of this, €54bn subsidised [agriculture enterprise](/wiki/Common_Agricultural_Policy "Common Agricultural Policy"), €42bn was spent on [transport](/wiki/Transport_in_the_European_Union "Transport in the European Union"), building and the environment, €16bn on [education and research](/wiki/Educational_policies_and_initiatives_of_the_European_Union "Educational policies and initiatives of the European Union"), €13bn on welfare, €20bn on foreign and defence policy, €2bn in [finance](/wiki/European_banking_union "European banking union"), €2bn in [energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_the_European_Union "Renewable energy in the European Union"), €1.5bn in communications, and €13bn in administration. In November 2020, two members of the union, Hungary and Poland, blocked approval to the EU's budget at a meeting in the [Committee of Permanent Representatives](/wiki/Committee_of_Permanent_Representatives "Committee of Permanent Representatives") (Coreper), citing a [proposal that linked funding with adherence to the rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_Law_Conditionality_Regulation "Rule of Law Conditionality Regulation"). The budget included a [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") recovery fund of €750 billion.[[119]](#cite_note-133)[[120]](#cite_note-134) The budget was finally approved by 12 December when Hungary and Poland withdrew their vetoes after further negotiations in the council and the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council").[[121]](#cite_note-135)

Bodies combatting fraud have also been established, including the [European Anti-fraud Office](/wiki/European_Anti-fraud_Office "European Anti-fraud Office") and the [European Public Prosecutor's Office](/wiki/European_Public_Prosecutor%27s_Office "European Public Prosecutor's Office"). The latter is a decentralised independent body of the European Union (EU), established under the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") between 22 of the 27 states of the EU following the method of [enhanced cooperation](/wiki/Enhanced_cooperation "Enhanced cooperation").[[122]](#cite_note-136) The European Public Prosecutor's Office investigate and prosecute fraud against the [budget of the European Union](/wiki/EU_budget "EU budget") and other crimes against the EU's financial interests including fraud concerning EU funds of over €10,000 and cross-border [VAT](/wiki/Value_added_tax "Value added tax") fraud cases involving damages above €10 million.

### Law

Main article: [European Union law](/wiki/European_Union_law "European Union law")

Further information: [European Union legislative procedure](/wiki/European_Union_legislative_procedure "European Union legislative procedure")

See also: [Treaties of the European Union](/wiki/Treaties_of_the_European_Union "Treaties of the European Union") and [European Citizens' Initiative](/wiki/European_Citizens%27_Initiative "European Citizens' Initiative")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Political_System_of_the_European_Union.svg/500px-Political_System_of_the_European_Union.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Political_System_of_the_European_Union.svg)

Organigram of the political system of the European Union

Constitutionally, the EU bears some resemblance to both a [confederation](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation") and a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation"),[[123]](#cite_note-137)[[124]](#cite_note-138) but has not formally defined itself as either. (It does not have a formal constitution: its status is defined by the [Treaty of European Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_European_Union "Treaty of European Union") and the [Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Functioning_of_the_European_Union "Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union")). It is more integrated than a traditional confederation of states because the general level of government widely employs [qualified majority voting](/wiki/Qualified_majority_voting "Qualified majority voting") in some decision-making among the member states, rather than relying exclusively on unanimity.[[125]](#cite_note-139)[[126]](#cite_note-140)

The EU is less integrated than a federal state because it is not a state in its own right: sovereignty continues to flow 'from the bottom up', from the several peoples of the separate member states, rather than from a single undifferentiated whole. This is reflected in the fact that the member states remain the 'masters of the Treaties', retaining control over the allocation of competences to the union through constitutional change (thus retaining so-called *[Kompetenz-kompetenz](/wiki/Kompetenz-kompetenz "Kompetenz-kompetenz")*); in that they retain control of the use of armed force; they retain control of taxation; and in that they retain a right of unilateral withdrawal under Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union. In addition, the principle of [subsidiarity](/wiki/Subsidiarity "Subsidiarity") requires that only those matters that need to be determined collectively are so determined.

Under the principle of [supremacy](/wiki/Supremacy_(European_Union_law) "Supremacy (European Union law)"), national courts are required to enforce the treaties that their member states have ratified, even if doing so requires them to ignore conflicting national law, and (within limits) even constitutional provisions.[[i]](#cite_note-Supremacy-141) The [direct effect](/wiki/Direct_effect "Direct effect") and supremacy doctrines were not explicitly set out in the European Treaties but were developed by the Court of Justice itself over the 1960s, apparently under the influence of its then most influential judge, Frenchman [Robert Lecourt](/wiki/Robert_Lecourt "Robert Lecourt").[[127]](#cite_note-142) The question whether the secondary law enacted by the EU has a comparable status in relation to national legislation, has been a matter of debate among legal scholars.

#### Primary law

The European Union is based on a series of [treaties](/wiki/Treaties_of_the_European_Union "Treaties of the European Union"). These first established the European Community and the EU, and then made amendments to those founding treaties.[[128]](#cite_note-143) These are power-giving treaties which set broad policy goals and establish institutions with the necessary legal powers to implement those goals. These legal powers include the ability to enact legislation[[j]](#cite_note-art249-144) which can directly affect all member states and their inhabitants.[[k]](#cite_note-Direct_Effect-145) The EU has [legal personality](/wiki/Legal_personality "Legal personality"), with the right to sign agreements and international treaties.[[129]](#cite_note-146)

#### Secondary law

The main legal acts of the European Union come in three forms: [regulations](/wiki/Regulation_(European_Union) "Regulation (European Union)"), [directives](/wiki/Directive_(European_Union) "Directive (European Union)"), and [decisions](/wiki/Decision_(European_Union) "Decision (European Union)"). Regulations become law in all member states the moment they come into force, without the requirement for any implementing measures,[[l]](#cite_note-147) and automatically override conflicting domestic provisions.[[j]](#cite_note-art249-144) Directives require member states to achieve a certain result while leaving them discretion as to how to achieve the result. The details of how they are to be implemented are left to member states.[[m]](#cite_note-148) When the time limit for implementing directives passes, they may, under certain conditions, have [direct effect](/wiki/Direct_effect "Direct effect") in national law against member states. Decisions offer an alternative to the two above modes of legislation. They are legal acts which only apply to specified individuals, companies or a particular member state. They are most often used in [competition law](/wiki/Competition_law "Competition law"), or on rulings on [State Aid](/wiki/State_aid_(European_Union) "State aid (European Union)"), but are also frequently used for procedural or administrative matters within the institutions. Regulations, directives, and decisions are of equal legal value and apply without any formal hierarchy.[[130]](#cite_note-149)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of the European Union](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_European_Union "Foreign relations of the European Union")

See also: [European External Action Service](/wiki/European_External_Action_Service "European External Action Service")

[![Portrait of Kaja Kallas](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Kaja_Kallas%2C_High_Representative_of_the_Union%2C_and_Vice-President_of_the_European_Commission_-_P064583-775481_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Kaja_Kallas%2C_High_Representative_of_the_Union%2C_and_Vice-President_of_the_European_Commission_-_P064583-775481_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaja_Kallas,_High_Representative_of_the_Union,_and_Vice-President_of_the_European_Commission_-_P064583-775481_(cropped).jpg)

[Kaja Kallas](/wiki/Kaja_Kallas "Kaja Kallas"),  
[High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy](/wiki/High_Representative_of_the_Union_for_Foreign_Affairs_and_Security_Policy "High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy")

Foreign policy co-operation between member states dates from the establishment of the community in 1957, when member states negotiated as a bloc in international trade negotiations under the [EU's common commercial policy](/wiki/EU%27s_common_commercial_policy "EU's common commercial policy").[[131]](#cite_note-150) Steps for more wide-ranging co-ordination in foreign relations began in 1970 with the establishment of [European Political Cooperation](/wiki/European_Political_Cooperation "European Political Cooperation") which created an informal consultation process between member states with the aim of forming common foreign policies. In 1987 the European Political Cooperation was introduced on a formal basis by the [Single European Act](/wiki/Single_European_Act "Single European Act"). EPC was renamed as the [Common Foreign and Security Policy](/wiki/Common_Foreign_and_Security_Policy "Common Foreign and Security Policy") (CFSP) by the [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty").[[132]](#cite_note-EPC-151)

The stated aims of the CFSP are to promote both the EU's own interests and those of the [international community](/wiki/International_community "International community") as a whole, including the furtherance of international co-operation, respect for human rights, democracy, and the rule of law.[[133]](#cite_note-art11_Union-152) The CFSP requires unanimity among the member states on the appropriate policy to follow on any particular issue. The unanimity and difficult issues treated under the CFSP sometimes lead to disagreements, such as those which occurred over the [war in Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War").[[134]](#cite_note-BBC_Iraq_statement-153)

The coordinator and representative of the CFSP within the EU is the [high representative of the union for foreign affairs and security policy](/wiki/High_Representative_of_the_Union_for_Foreign_Affairs_and_Security_Policy "High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy") who speaks on behalf of the EU in foreign policy and defence matters, and has the task of articulating the positions expressed by the member states on these fields of policy into a common alignment. The high representative heads up the [European External Action Service](/wiki/European_External_Action_Service "European External Action Service") (EEAS), a unique EU department[[135]](#cite_note-23Oct-154) that has been officially implemented and operational since 1 December 2010 on the occasion of the first anniversary of the entry into force of the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon").[[136]](#cite_note-155) The EEAS serves as a foreign ministry and [diplomatic corps](/wiki/Diplomatic_corps "Diplomatic corps") for the European Union.[[137]](#cite_note-Europa_EEAS-156)

Besides the emerging international policy of the European Union, the international influence of the EU is also felt through [enlargement](/wiki/Enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Enlargement of the European Union"). The perceived benefits of becoming a member of the EU act as an incentive for both political and economic reform in states wishing to fulfil the EU's accession criteria, and are considered an important factor contributing to the reform of European formerly Communist countries.[[138]](#cite_note-157): 762 This influence on the internal affairs of other countries is generally referred to as "[soft power](/wiki/Soft_power "Soft power")", as opposed to military "hard power".[[139]](#cite_note-Soft_Power-158)

#### Humanitarian aid

Further information: [Directorate-General for European Civil Protection and Humanitarian Aid Operations](/wiki/Directorate-General_for_European_Civil_Protection_and_Humanitarian_Aid_Operations "Directorate-General for European Civil Protection and Humanitarian Aid Operations")

The [European Commission's Humanitarian Aid and Civil Protection department](/wiki/ECHO_(European_Commission) "ECHO (European Commission)"), or "ECHO", provides [humanitarian aid](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid "Humanitarian aid") from the EU to [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country"). In 2012, its budget amounted to €874 million, 51 per cent of the budget went to Africa and 20 per cent to Asia, Latin America, the Caribbean and Pacific, and 20 per cent to the Middle East and Mediterranean.[[140]](#cite_note-159) Humanitarian aid is financed directly by the budget (70 per cent) as part of the financial instruments for external action and also by the [European Development Fund](/wiki/European_Development_Fund "European Development Fund") (30 per cent).[[141]](#cite_note-ODI-160) The EU's external action financing is divided into geographic instruments and thematic instruments.[[141]](#cite_note-ODI-160) The geographic instruments provide aid through the [Development Cooperation Instrument](/wiki/Development_Cooperation_Instrument "Development Cooperation Instrument") (DCI, €16.9 billion, 2007–2013), which must spend 95 per cent of its budget on [official development assistance](/wiki/Official_development_assistance "Official development assistance") (ODA), and from the [European Neighbourhood and Partnership Instrument](/wiki/European_Neighbourhood_and_Partnership_Instrument "European Neighbourhood and Partnership Instrument") (ENPI), which contains some relevant programmes.[[141]](#cite_note-ODI-160)

The European Development Fund (EDF, €22.7 billion for the period 2008–2013 and €30.5 billion for the period 2014–2020) is made up of voluntary contributions by member states, but there is pressure to merge the EDF into the budget-financed instruments to encourage increased contributions to match the 0.7 per cent target and allow the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") greater oversight.[[141]](#cite_note-ODI-160)[[142]](#cite_note-161) In 2016, the average among EU countries was 0.4 per cent and five had met or exceeded the 0.7 per cent target: Denmark, Germany, Luxembourg, Sweden, and the United Kingdom.[[143]](#cite_note-162)

#### International cooperation and development partnerships

Main articles: [Directorate-General for International Partnerships](/wiki/Directorate-General_for_International_Partnerships "Directorate-General for International Partnerships"), [ACP–EU development cooperation](/wiki/ACP%E2%80%93EU_development_cooperation "ACP–EU development cooperation"), [European Solidarity Corps](/wiki/European_Solidarity_Corps "European Solidarity Corps"), [European Union Global Strategy](/wiki/European_Union_Global_Strategy "European Union Global Strategy"), [European Neighbourhood Policy](/wiki/European_Neighbourhood_Policy "European Neighbourhood Policy"), [Global Europe](/wiki/Global_Europe "Global Europe"), and [European Political Community](/wiki/European_Political_Community "European Political Community")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Ilham_Aliyev_attended_EU_Eastern_Partnership_Summit_in_Brussels.jpg/250px-Ilham_Aliyev_attended_EU_Eastern_Partnership_Summit_in_Brussels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ilham_Aliyev_attended_EU_Eastern_Partnership_Summit_in_Brussels.jpg)

[Eastern Partnership](/wiki/Eastern_Partnership "Eastern Partnership") Summit 2017, [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")

The European Union uses foreign relations instruments like the [European Neighbourhood Policy](/wiki/European_Neighbourhood_Policy "European Neighbourhood Policy") which seeks to tie those countries to the east and south of the European territory of the EU to the union. These countries, primarily developing countries, include some who seek to one day become either a [member state of the European Union](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union"), or more closely integrated with the European Union. The EU offers financial assistance to countries within the European Neighbourhood, so long as they meet the strict conditions of government reform, economic reform and other issues surrounding positive transformation. This process is normally underpinned by an Action Plan, as agreed by both Brussels and the target country.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Membreupm.png/250px-Membreupm.png)](/wiki/File:Membreupm.png)

[Union for the Mediterranean](/wiki/Union_for_the_Mediterranean "Union for the Mediterranean") meeting in Barcelona

There is also the worldwide [European Union Global Strategy](/wiki/European_Union_Global_Strategy "European Union Global Strategy"). International recognition of sustainable development as a key element is growing steadily. Its role was recognised in three major UN summits on sustainable development: the 1992 [UN Conference on Environment and Development](/wiki/Earth_Summit "Earth Summit") (UNCED) in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil; the 2002 World Summit on Sustainable Development (WSSD) in Johannesburg, [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"); and the 2012 [UN Conference on Sustainable Development](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_Sustainable_Development "United Nations Conference on Sustainable Development") (UNCSD) in Rio de Janeiro. Other key global agreements are the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") and the [2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals") (United Nations, 2015). The SDGs recognise that all countries must stimulate action in the following key areas – people, [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), prosperity, [peace](/wiki/Peace "Peace") and [partnership](/wiki/Partnership "Partnership") – in order to tackle the global challenges that are crucial for the survival of [humanity](/wiki/Human "Human").

EU development action is based on the European Consensus on Development, which was endorsed on 20 December 2005 by EU Member States, the council, the European Parliament and the commission.[[144]](#cite_note-163) It is applied from the principles of [capability approach](/wiki/Capability_approach "Capability approach") and [rights-based approach to development](/wiki/Rights-based_approach_to_development "Rights-based approach to development"). Funding is provided by the [Instrument for Pre-Accession Assistance](/wiki/Instrument_for_Pre-Accession_Assistance "Instrument for Pre-Accession Assistance") and the [Global Europe](/wiki/Global_Europe "Global Europe") programmes. Partnership and cooperation agreements are bilateral agreements with non-member nations.[[145]](#cite_note-pca-164)

### Defence

Main article: [Common Security and Defence Policy](/wiki/Common_Security_and_Defence_Policy "Common Security and Defence Policy")

See also: [European Union–NATO relations](/wiki/European_Union%E2%80%93NATO_relations "European Union–NATO relations"), [Frontex](/wiki/Frontex "Frontex"), [European Defence Agency](/wiki/European_Defence_Agency "European Defence Agency"), [European Union Institute for Security Studies](/wiki/European_Union_Institute_for_Security_Studies "European Union Institute for Security Studies"), and [European Union Satellite Centre](/wiki/European_Union_Satellite_Centre "European Union Satellite Centre")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/EU_and_NATO.svg/250px-EU_and_NATO.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EU_and_NATO.svg)

Map showing European membership of the EU and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")

EU member only

NATO member only

EU and NATO member

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Coat_of_arms_of_the_European_Union_Military_Staff.svg/250px-Coat_of_arms_of_the_European_Union_Military_Staff.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_the_European_Union_Military_Staff.svg)

Coat of arms of the [Military Staff](/wiki/European_Union_Military_Staff "European Union Military Staff")

The predecessors of the European Union were not devised as a military alliance because [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") was largely seen as appropriate and sufficient for defence purposes.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2007100-165) In 2025, Europe initiated the [ReArm program](/wiki/ReArm_Europe "ReArm Europe"), a strategic breakthrough aimed at mobilising local industrial capabilities and bolstering European equipment production, involves a financial investment of €800 billion to support the development and procurement, simultaneously enhancing the continent's overall military readiness and self-sufficiency.[[147]](#cite_note-166)[[148]](#cite_note-167)[[149]](#cite_note-168)

Twenty-three EU members are members of NATO while the remaining member states follow policies of [neutrality](/wiki/Neutrality_(international_relations) "Neutrality (international relations)").[[150]](#cite_note-169) The [Western European Union](/wiki/Western_European_Union "Western European Union"), a military alliance with a mutual defence clause, closed in 2011[[151]](#cite_note-170) as its role had been transferred to the EU.[[152]](#cite_note-171) Following the [Kosovo War](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War") in 1999, the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") agreed that "the Union must have the capacity for autonomous action, backed by credible military forces, the means to decide to use them, and the readiness to do so, in order to respond to international crises without prejudice to actions by NATO". To that end, a number of efforts were made to increase the EU's military capability, notably the [Helsinki Headline Goal](/wiki/Helsinki_Headline_Goal "Helsinki Headline Goal") process. After much discussion, the most concrete result was the [EU Battlegroups](/wiki/EU_Battlegroup "EU Battlegroup") initiative, each of which is planned to be able to deploy quickly about 1500 personnel.[[153]](#cite_note-Council_Capabilities-172) The EU Strategic Compass adopted in 2022 reaffirmed the bloc's partnership with NATO, committed to increased military mobility and formation of a 5,000-strong EU Rapid Deployment Capacity[[154]](#cite_note-173)

Since the withdrawal of the United Kingdom, France is the only member officially recognised as a [nuclear weapon state](/wiki/List_of_states_with_nuclear_weapons "List of states with nuclear weapons") and the sole holder of a [permanent seat](/wiki/Permanent_members_of_the_United_Nations_Security_Council "Permanent members of the United Nations Security Council") on the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council"). France and Italy are also the only EU countries that have [power projection capabilities](/wiki/Power_projection "Power projection") outside of Europe.[[155]](#cite_note-174) Italy, Germany, the Netherlands and Belgium participate in NATO [nuclear sharing](/wiki/Nuclear_sharing "Nuclear sharing").[[156]](#cite_note-175) Most EU member states opposed the [Nuclear Weapon Ban Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[157]](#cite_note-176)

[EU forces](/wiki/EUFOR "EUFOR") have been deployed on peacekeeping missions from middle and northern Africa to the [western Balkans](/wiki/Western_Balkans "Western Balkans") and western Asia.[[158]](#cite_note-Council_Operations-177) EU military operations are supported by a number of bodies, including the [European Defence Agency](/wiki/European_Defence_Agency "European Defence Agency"), [European Union Satellite Centre](/wiki/European_Union_Satellite_Centre "European Union Satellite Centre") and the [European Union Military Staff](/wiki/European_Union_Military_Staff "European Union Military Staff").[[159]](#cite_note-Council_Structures-178) The European Union Military Staff is the highest military institution of the European Union, established within the framework of the European Council, and follows on from the decisions of the Helsinki European Council (10–11 December 1999), which called for the establishment of permanent political-military institutions. The European Union Military Staff is under the authority of the [High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy](/wiki/High_Representative_of_the_Union_for_Foreign_Affairs_and_Security_Policy "High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy") and the Political and Security Committee. It directs all military activities in the EU context, including planning and conducting military missions and operations in the framework of the [Common Security and Defence Policy](/wiki/Common_Security_and_Defence_Policy "Common Security and Defence Policy") and the development of military capabilities, and provides the Political and Security Committee with military advice and recommendations on military issues. In an EU consisting of 27 members, substantial security and defence co-operation is increasingly relying on collaboration among all member states.[[160]](#cite_note-179)

The European Border and Coast Guard Agency ([Frontex](/wiki/Frontex "Frontex")) is an [agency of the EU](/wiki/Agencies_of_the_European_Union "Agencies of the European Union") aiming to detect and stop illegal immigration, [human trafficking](/wiki/Human_trafficking "Human trafficking") and terrorist infiltration.[[161]](#cite_note-180) The EU also operates the [European Travel Information and Authorisation System](/wiki/European_Travel_Information_and_Authorisation_System "European Travel Information and Authorisation System"), the [Entry/Exit System](/wiki/Entry/Exit_System "Entry/Exit System"), the [Schengen Information System](/wiki/Schengen_Information_System "Schengen Information System"), the [Visa Information System](/wiki/Visa_Information_System "Visa Information System") and the [Common European Asylum System](/wiki/Common_European_Asylum_System "Common European Asylum System") which provide common databases for police and immigration authorities. The impetus for the development of this co-operation was the advent of open borders in the Schengen Area and the associated cross-border crime.[[18]](#cite_note-Internal_borders-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/European_Union_relief_laea_location_map.svg/250px-European_Union_relief_laea_location_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:European_Union_relief_laea_location_map.svg)

Topographic map of Europe (EU highlighted)

The EU's member states cover an area of 4,233,262 square kilometres (1,634,472 sq mi),[[n]](#cite_note-Area.and.population.figure-181) and therefore a large part of the [European continent](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). The EU's highest peak is [Mont Blanc](/wiki/Mont_Blanc "Mont Blanc") in the [Graian Alps](/wiki/Graian_Alps "Graian Alps"), 4,810.45 metres (15,782 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").[[162]](#cite_note-182) The lowest points in the EU are [Lammefjorden](/wiki/Lammefjorden "Lammefjorden"), Denmark, and [Zuidplaspolder](/wiki/Zuidplaspolder "Zuidplaspolder"), Netherlands, at 7 m (23 ft) below sea level.[[69]](#cite_note-CIA-81) The landscape, climate, and economy of the EU are influenced by its coastline, which is 65,993 kilometres (41,006 mi) long. In addition to national territories in Europe, there are 32 [special territories of members of the European Economic Area](/wiki/Special_territories_of_members_of_the_European_Economic_Area "Special territories of members of the European Economic Area"), not all of which are part of the EU. The largest by area is [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), which is [not part of the EU but whose citizens are EU citizens](/wiki/Greenland_and_the_European_Union "Greenland and the European Union"), while the largest by population are the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands") off Africa, which are part of the EU and the [Schengen area](/wiki/Schengen_area "Schengen area"). [French Guiana](/wiki/French_Guiana "French Guiana") in South America is part of the EU and the eurozone, as is [Mayotte](/wiki/Mayotte "Mayotte"), north of [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Koppen-Geiger_Map_Europe_present.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_Europe_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_Europe_present.svg)

A [Köppen–Geiger climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map of Europe (including non-EU member states)

The climate of the European Union is of a [temperate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate"), [continental](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") nature, with a [maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate") prevailing on the western coasts and a [mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") in the south. The climate is strongly conditioned by the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream"), which warms the western region to levels unattainable at similar latitudes on other continents. Western Europe is oceanic, while eastern Europe is continental and dry. Four seasons occur in western Europe, while southern Europe experiences a [wet season](/wiki/Wet_season "Wet season") and a [dry season](/wiki/Dry_season "Dry season"). Southern Europe is hot and dry during the summer months. The heaviest precipitation occurs downwind of water bodies due to the prevailing [westerlies](/wiki/Westerlies "Westerlies"), with higher amounts also seen in the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps").

### Environment

Main article: [Climate change in Europe](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Europe "Climate change in Europe")

See also: [European Environment Agency](/wiki/European_Environment_Agency "European Environment Agency") and [Environmental policy of the European Union](/wiki/Environmental_policy_of_the_European_Union "Environmental policy of the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Increase_of_average_yearly_temperature_in_Europe_%281900-2017%29.png/250px-Increase_of_average_yearly_temperature_in_Europe_%281900-2017%29.png)](/wiki/File:Increase_of_average_yearly_temperature_in_Europe_(1900-2017).png)

Increase of average yearly temperature in selected cities in Europe (1900–2017)[[163]](#cite_note-183)

In 1957, when the European Economic Community was founded, it had no environmental policy.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJordanAdelle2012-184) Over the past 50 years, an increasingly dense network of legislation has been created, extending to all areas of environmental protection, including air pollution, water quality, waste management, nature conservation, and the control of chemicals, industrial hazards, and biotechnology.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJordanAdelle2012-184) According to the [Institute for European Environmental Policy](/wiki/Institute_for_European_Environmental_Policy "Institute for European Environmental Policy"), environmental law comprises over 500 Directives, Regulations and Decisions, making environmental policy a core area of European politics.[[165]](#cite_note-European_Environmental_Policy_2012-185)

European policy-makers originally increased the EU's capacity to act on environmental issues by defining it as a trade problem.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJordanAdelle2012-184) [Trade barriers](/wiki/Trade_barrier "Trade barrier") and competitive distortions in the Common Market could emerge due to the different environmental standards in each member state.[[166]](#cite_note-186) In subsequent years, the environment became a formal policy area, with its own policy actors, principles and procedures. The legal basis for EU environmental policy was established with the introduction of the Single European Act in 1987.[[165]](#cite_note-European_Environmental_Policy_2012-185)

Initially, EU environmental policy focused on Europe. More recently, the EU has demonstrated leadership in global environmental governance, e.g. the role of the EU in securing the ratification and coming into force of the [Kyoto Protocol](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol") despite opposition from the United States. This international dimension is reflected in the EU's Sixth Environmental Action Programme,[[167]](#cite_note-187) which recognises that its objectives can only be achieved if key international agreements are actively supported and properly implemented both at EU level and worldwide. The Lisbon Treaty further strengthened the leadership ambitions.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJordanAdelle2012-184) EU law has played a significant role in improving habitat and species protection in Europe, as well as contributing to improvements in air and water quality and waste management.[[165]](#cite_note-European_Environmental_Policy_2012-185)

Mitigating [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") is one of the top priorities of EU environmental policy. In 2007, member states agreed that, in the future, 20 per cent of the energy used across the EU must be [renewable](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy"), and carbon dioxide emissions have to be lower in 2020 by at least 20 per cent compared to 1990 levels.[[168]](#cite_note-EUO_energy-188) In 2017, the EU emitted 9.1 per cent of global [greenhouse-gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse-gas_emissions "Greenhouse-gas emissions").[[169]](#cite_note-Global_Emissions-189) The European Union claims that already in 2018, its GHG emissions were 23% lower than in 1990.[[170]](#cite_note-190)

The EU has adopted an [emissions trading system](/wiki/Emissions_trading "Emissions trading") to incorporate [carbon emissions](/wiki/Carbon_emissions "Carbon emissions") into the economy.[[171]](#cite_note-191) The [European Green Capital](/wiki/European_Green_Capital "European Green Capital") is an annual award given to cities that focuses on the environment, energy efficiency, and quality of life in urban areas to create [smart city](/wiki/Smart_city "Smart city"). In the [2019 elections to the European Parliament](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election "2019 European Parliament election"), the green parties increased their power, possibly because of the rise of post materialist values.[[172]](#cite_note-192) Proposals to reach a zero carbon economy in the European Union by 2050 were suggested in 2018–2019. Almost all member states supported that goal at an EU summit in June 2019. The Czech Republic, Estonia, Hungary, and Poland disagreed.[[173]](#cite_note-193) In June 2021, the European Union passed a [European Climate Law](/w/index.php?title=European_Climate_Law&action=edit&redlink=1 "European Climate Law (page does not exist)") with targets of 55% GHG emissions reduction by 2030 and [carbon neutrality](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") by 2050.[[174]](#cite_note-194) Also in the same year, the European Union and the United States pledged to cut [methane emissions](/wiki/Methane_emissions "Methane emissions") by 30% by 2030. The pledge is considered as a big achievement for climate change mitigation.[[175]](#cite_note-195) A research report from November 2024 declared that the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") is the EU's most toxic country in Europe for car emissions.[[176]](#cite_note-196)

In 2025, The European Union together with [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and others, joined the [Global carbon market coalition](/wiki/Climate_Coalition_(COP30_proposal) "Climate Coalition (COP30 proposal)").[[177]](#cite_note-197) According to some calculations, a global carbon market can speed up emission reduction seven-fold.[[178]](#cite_note-198)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of the European Union](/wiki/Economy_of_the_European_Union "Economy of the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Europe-GDP-PPP-per-capita-map.png/250px-Europe-GDP-PPP-per-capita-map.png)](/wiki/File:Europe-GDP-PPP-per-capita-map.png)

[GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") (PPP) per capita (including non-EU countries)

The [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP), a measure of economic activity, of EU member states was US$16.64 trillion in 2022, around 16.6 per cent of the world GDP.[[179]](#cite_note-199) There is a significant variation in GDP per capita between and within individual EU states. The difference between the richest and poorest regions (281 NUTS-2 regions of the [Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics](/wiki/Nomenclature_of_Territorial_Units_for_Statistics "Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics")) ranged, in 2017, from 31 per cent (Severozapaden, Bulgaria) of the EU28 average (€30,000) to 253 per cent (Luxembourg), or from €4,600 to €92,600.[[180]](#cite_note-200)

EU member states own the estimated third largest after the United States (US$140 trillion) and [China](/wiki/China "China") (US$84 trillion) [net wealth](/wiki/Net_wealth "Net wealth") in the world, equal to around one sixth (US$76 trillion) of the US$454 trillion global wealth.[[181]](#cite_note-databook2023-201) Of the top [500 largest corporations in the world measured by revenue](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500 "Fortune Global 500") in 2024, 90 had their headquarters in the EU.[[182]](#cite_note-202) In 2016, unemployment in the EU stood at 8.9 per cent[[183]](#cite_note-UNEMP-203) while inflation was at 2.2 per cent, and the account balance at −0.9 per cent of GDP. The average annual net earnings in the European Union was around €25,000[[184]](#cite_note-204) in 2021.

### Economic and monetary union

Main article: [Economic and Monetary Union of the European Union](/wiki/Economic_and_Monetary_Union_of_the_European_Union "Economic and Monetary Union of the European Union")

The [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") is the official currency in 21 member states of the EU. The creation of a [European single currency](/wiki/European_Currency_Unit "European Currency Unit") became an official objective of the European Economic Community in 1969. In 1992, having negotiated the structure and procedures of a currency union, the member states signed the [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty") and were legally bound to fulfil the agreed-on rules including the [convergence criteria](/wiki/Euro_convergence_criteria "Euro convergence criteria") if they wanted to join the [monetary union](/wiki/Currency_union "Currency union"). The states wanting to participate had first to join the [European Exchange Rate Mechanism](/wiki/European_Exchange_Rate_Mechanism "European Exchange Rate Mechanism"). To prevent the joining states from getting into financial trouble or crisis after entering the monetary union, they were obliged in the Maastricht treaty to fulfil important financial obligations and procedures, especially to show budgetary discipline and a high degree of sustainable economic convergence, as well as to avoid excessive government deficits and limit the government debt to a sustainable level, as agreed in the [European Fiscal Pact](/wiki/European_Fiscal_Pact "European Fiscal Pact").

#### Capital Markets Union and financial institutions

Main articles: [Capital Markets Union](/wiki/Capital_Markets_Union "Capital Markets Union"), [European System of Financial Supervision](/wiki/European_System_of_Financial_Supervision "European System of Financial Supervision"), and [European Stability Mechanism](/wiki/European_Stability_Mechanism "European Stability Mechanism")

See also: [European Banking Authority](/wiki/European_Banking_Authority "European Banking Authority"), [European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority](/wiki/European_Insurance_and_Occupational_Pensions_Authority "European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority"), [European Securities and Markets Authority](/wiki/European_Securities_and_Markets_Authority "European Securities and Markets Authority"), and [Single Resolution Board](/wiki/Single_Resolution_Board "Single Resolution Board")

Free movement of capital is intended to permit movement of investments such as property purchases and buying of shares between countries.[[185]](#cite_note-Europa_Single_Market_C-205) Until the drive towards [economic and monetary union](/wiki/Economic_and_monetary_union "Economic and monetary union") the development of the capital provisions had been slow. Post-Maastricht there has been a rapidly developing corpus of ECJ judgements regarding this initially neglected freedom. The free movement of capital is unique insofar as it is granted equally to non-member states.

The [European System of Financial Supervision](/wiki/European_System_of_Financial_Supervision "European System of Financial Supervision") is an institutional architecture of the EU's framework of financial supervision composed by three authorities: the [European Banking Authority](/wiki/European_Banking_Authority "European Banking Authority"), the [European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority](/wiki/European_Insurance_and_Occupational_Pensions_Authority "European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority") and the [European Securities and Markets Authority](/wiki/European_Securities_and_Markets_Authority "European Securities and Markets Authority"). To complement this framework, there is also a [European Systemic Risk Board](/wiki/European_Systemic_Risk_Board "European Systemic Risk Board") under the responsibility of the central bank. The aim of this financial control system is to ensure the economic stability of the EU.[[186]](#cite_note-206)

#### Eurozone and banking union

Main articles: [Euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"), [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone"), and [European banking union](/wiki/European_banking_union "European banking union")

See also: [Eurosystem](/wiki/Eurosystem "Eurosystem") and [Eurogroup](/wiki/Eurogroup "Eurogroup")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Euro_banknotes%2C_Europa_series.png/250px-Euro_banknotes%2C_Europa_series.png)](/wiki/File:Euro_banknotes,_Europa_series.png)

[Euro banknotes](/wiki/Euro_banknotes "Euro banknotes") from the Europa series (since 2013)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Seat_of_the_ECB%2C_Southwest_view_20220213_1.jpg/250px-Seat_of_the_ECB%2C_Southwest_view_20220213_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seat_of_the_ECB,_Southwest_view_20220213_1.jpg)

[European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") in [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), Germany

In 1999, the currency union started to materialise through introducing a common accounting (virtual) currency in [eleven of the member states](/wiki/History_of_the_euro "History of the euro"). In 2002, it was turned into a fully-fledged conventible currency, when [euro notes](/wiki/Euro_banknotes "Euro banknotes") and coins were issued, while the phaseout of national currencies in the eurozone (consisting by then of 12 member states) was initiated. The eurozone (constituted by the EU member states which have adopted the euro) has since grown to 20 countries.[[187]](#cite_note-207)[[188]](#cite_note-208)

The 20 EU member states known collectively as the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") have fully implemented the currency union by superseding their national currencies with the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"). The currency union represents 345 million EU citizens.[[14]](#cite_note-Europa_Internal_Market-20) The euro is the second largest [reserve currency](/wiki/Reserve_currency "Reserve currency") as well as the second most traded currency in the world after the [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar").[[189]](#cite_note-209)[[190]](#cite_note-210)[[191]](#cite_note-211)

The euro, and the monetary policies of those who have adopted it in agreement with the EU, are under the control of the ECB.[[192]](#cite_note-ECB_org-212) The ECB is the central bank for the eurozone, and thus controls [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") in that area with an agenda to maintain [price stability](/wiki/Price_stability "Price stability"). It is at the centre of the [Eurosystem](/wiki/Eurosystem "Eurosystem"), which comprehends all the eurozone national central banks.[[192]](#cite_note-ECB_org-212) The ECB is also the central institution of the [Banking Union](/wiki/Banking_Union "Banking Union") established within the eurozone, as the hub of [European Banking Supervision](/wiki/European_Banking_Supervision "European Banking Supervision"). There is also a [Single Resolution Mechanism](/wiki/Single_Resolution_Mechanism "Single Resolution Mechanism") in case of a bank default.

### Trade

As a political entity, the European Union is represented in the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO). Two of the original core objectives of the European Economic Community were the development of a common market, subsequently becoming a [single market](/wiki/Single_market "Single market"), and a [customs union](/wiki/European_Union_Customs_Union "European Union Customs Union") between its member states.

#### Single market

Main article: [European single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market")

Further information: [Digital Single Market](/wiki/Digital_Single_Market "Digital Single Market")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/EU_Single_Market.svg/250px-EU_Single_Market.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EU_Single_Market.svg)

[European Single Market](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market")

[EU member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union")

Non-EU states which participate

The single market involves [the free circulation of goods, capital, people, and services within the EU](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market"),[[14]](#cite_note-Europa_Internal_Market-20) The free movement of services and of establishment allows self-employed persons to move between member states to provide services on a temporary or permanent basis. While services account for 60 per cent to 70 per cent of GDP, legislation in the area is not as developed as in other areas. This lacuna has been addressed by the [Services in the Internal Market Directive 2006](/wiki/Services_in_the_Internal_Market_Directive_2006 "Services in the Internal Market Directive 2006") which aims to liberalise the cross border provision of services.[[193]](#cite_note-Europa_Single_Market_S-213) According to the treaty the provision of services is a residual freedom that only applies if no other freedom is being exercised.

#### Customs union

Main article: [European Union Customs Union](/wiki/European_Union_Customs_Union "European Union Customs Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/EU_Customs_Union.svg/250px-EU_Customs_Union.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EU_Customs_Union.svg)

[European Customs Union](/wiki/European_Customs_Union "European Customs Union")

[EU member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union")

Non-EU states which participate

The customs union involves the application of a [common external tariff](/wiki/Common_external_tariff "Common external tariff") on all goods entering the market. Once goods have been admitted into the market they cannot be subjected to customs duties, discriminatory taxes or [import quotas](/wiki/Import_quota "Import quota"), as they travel internally. The non-EU member states of [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") participate in the single market but not in the customs union.[[75]](#cite_note-EEA-88) Half the trade in the EU is covered by legislation harmonised by the EU.[[194]](#cite_note-Europa_Single_Market_G-214)

The [European Union Association Agreement](/wiki/European_Union_Association_Agreement "European Union Association Agreement") does something similar for a much larger range of countries, partly as a so-called soft approach ('a carrot instead of a stick') to influence the politics in those countries.
The European Union represents all its members at the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO), and acts on behalf of member states in any disputes. When the EU negotiates trade related agreement outside the WTO framework, the subsequent agreement must be approved by each individual EU member state government.[[195]](#cite_note-Se-jeong-215)

#### Competition and consumer protection

Main articles: [European Union competition law](/wiki/European_Union_competition_law "European Union competition law") and [European consumer law](/wiki/European_consumer_law "European consumer law")

See also: [European Union Intellectual Property Office](/wiki/European_Union_Intellectual_Property_Office "European Union Intellectual Property Office")

The EU operates a [competition policy](/wiki/European_Union_competition_law "European Union competition law") intended to ensure undistorted competition within the single market.[[o]](#cite_note-216) In 2001 the commission for the first time prevented a merger between two companies based in the United States ([General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") and [Honeywell](/wiki/Honeywell "Honeywell")) which had already been approved by their national authority.[[196]](#cite_note-217) Another high-profile case, [against Microsoft](/wiki/European_Union_Microsoft_competition_case "European Union Microsoft competition case"), resulted in the commission fining [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") over €777 million following nine years of legal action.[[197]](#cite_note-218)

#### External trade

Main article: [Common Commercial Policy (EU)](/wiki/Common_Commercial_Policy_(EU) "Common Commercial Policy (EU)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/EU_FTAs.svg/250px-EU_FTAs.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EU_FTAs.svg)

EU free trade agreements

European Union

Agreement in force

Agreement (in part) provisionally applied

The European Union has concluded [free trade agreements](/wiki/European_Union_free_trade_agreements "European Union free trade agreements") (FTAs)[[198]](#cite_note-219) and other agreements with a trade component with many countries worldwide and is negotiating with many others.[[199]](#cite_note-220) The European Union's services trade surplus rose from $16 billion in 2000 to more than $250 billion in 2018.[[200]](#cite_note-221) In 2020, in part due to the COVID-19 pandemic, China became the EU's largest trading partner, displacing the United States.[[201]](#cite_note-222) The European Union is the largest exporter in the world[[202]](#cite_note-223) and in 2008 was the largest importer of goods and services.[[203]](#cite_note-224)[[204]](#cite_note-225) Internal trade between the member states is aided by the removal of barriers to trade such as [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") and [border controls](/wiki/Border_control "Border control"). In the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone"), trade is helped by not having any currency differences to deal with amongst most members.[[195]](#cite_note-Se-jeong-215) Externally, the EU's free-trade agreement with [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") is perhaps its most notable one. The [EU-Japan Economic Partnership Agreement](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93European_Union_relations#Political_relations_and_agreements "Japan–European Union relations") was officially signed on 17 July 2018, becoming the world's largest bilateral free trade deal when it went into effect on 1 February 2019, creating an open trade zone covering nearly one-third of global GDP.[[205]](#cite_note-226)[[206]](#cite_note-227)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy policy of the European Union](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_the_European_Union "Energy policy of the European Union")

See also: [European Union Agency for the Cooperation of Energy Regulators](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_the_Cooperation_of_Energy_Regulators "European Union Agency for the Cooperation of Energy Regulators")

Total energy supply (2019)[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228)

1. Oil (31.7%)
2. Natural gas (24.7%)
3. Coal (10.9%)
4. Nuclear (13.2%)
5. Biofuels, waste, electricity, heat (19.4%)

The total energy supply of the EU was 59 billion [GJ](/wiki/Joule_(unit) "Joule (unit)") in 2019, about 10.2 per cent of the world total. Approximately three fifths of the energy available in the EU came from imports (mostly of fossil fuels). [Renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") contributed 18.1 per cent of the EU's total energy supply in 2019, and 11.1 per cent of the final energy consumption.[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228) The EU has had legislative power in the area of energy policy for most of its existence; this has its roots in the original [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community"). The introduction of a mandatory and comprehensive European energy policy was approved at the meeting of the European Council in October 2005, and the first draft policy was published in January 2007.[[208]](#cite_note-Energy_Q&A-229)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Energy_Community_Map.svg/250px-Energy_Community_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Energy_Community_Map.svg)

[Energy Community](/wiki/Energy_Community "Energy Community")

The EU has five key points in its energy policy: increase competition in the [internal market](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market"), encourage investment and boost interconnections between electricity grids; diversify energy resources with better systems to respond to a crisis; establish a new treaty framework for energy co-operation with Russia while improving relations with energy-rich states in Central Asia[[209]](#cite_note-oies-230) and North Africa; use existing energy supplies more efficiently while increasing [renewable energy commercialisation](/wiki/Renewable_energy_commercialisation "Renewable energy commercialisation"); and finally increase funding for new energy technologies.[[208]](#cite_note-Energy_Q&A-229)

In 2007, EU countries as a whole imported 82 per cent of their oil, 57 per cent of their natural gas[[210]](#cite_note-low_carb_prop-231) and 97.48 per cent of their uranium[[211]](#cite_note-Euratom2007-232) demands. The three largest suppliers of natural gas to the European Union are Russia, Norway and [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"), that amounted for about three quarters of the imports in 2019.[[212]](#cite_note-233) There is a strong [dependence on Russian energy](/wiki/Russia_in_the_European_energy_sector "Russia in the European energy sector") that the EU has been attempting to reduce.[[213]](#cite_note-Energy_Russia-234)

In May 2022, it was reported that the European Union was preparing another sanction against Russia over its invasion of Ukraine. It is expected to target Russian oil, Russian and Belarusian banks, as well as individuals and companies. According to an article by Reuters, two diplomats stated that the European Union may impose a ban on imports of Russian oil by the end of 2022.[[214]](#cite_note-235) Also in May 2022, the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") published the RePowerEU initiative, a €300 billion plan outlining the path towards the end of EU dependence on Russian fossil fuels by 2030 and the acceleration on the clean energy transition.[[215]](#cite_note-236)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in the European Union](/wiki/Transport_in_the_European_Union "Transport in the European Union")

Further information: [Trans-European Transport Network](/wiki/Trans-European_Transport_Network "Trans-European Transport Network")

See also: [European Union Aviation Safety Agency](/wiki/European_Union_Aviation_Safety_Agency "European Union Aviation Safety Agency"), [European Maritime Safety Agency](/wiki/European_Maritime_Safety_Agency "European Maritime Safety Agency"), and [European Union Agency for Railways](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_Railways "European Union Agency for Railways")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Trans-European_Transport_Network_%282024%29.png/250px-Trans-European_Transport_Network_%282024%29.png)](/wiki/File:Trans-European_Transport_Network_(2024).png)

Map of the [Trans-European Transport Network](/wiki/Trans-European_Transport_Network "Trans-European Transport Network")

The European Union manages cross-border road, railway, airport and water infrastructure through the [Trans-European Transport Network](/wiki/Trans-European_Transport_Network "Trans-European Transport Network") (TEN-T), created in 1990,[[216]](#cite_note-Axes_page_7-237) and the [Trans-European Combined Transport network](/wiki/Trans-European_Combined_Transport_network "Trans-European Combined Transport network"). TEN-T comprises two network layers: the Core Network, which is to be completed by 2030; and the Comprehensive Network, which is to be completed by 2050. The network is currently made up of 9 core corridors: the [Baltic–Adriatic Corridor](/wiki/Baltic%E2%80%93Adriatic_Corridor "Baltic–Adriatic Corridor"), the [North Sea–Baltic Corridor](/wiki/North_Sea%E2%80%93Baltic_Corridor "North Sea–Baltic Corridor"), the [Mediterranean Corridor](/wiki/Mediterranean_Corridor "Mediterranean Corridor"), the [Orient/East–Med Corridor](/wiki/Orient/East%E2%80%93Med_Corridor "Orient/East–Med Corridor"), the [Scandinavian–Mediterranean Corridor](/wiki/Scandinavian%E2%80%93Mediterranean_Corridor "Scandinavian–Mediterranean Corridor"), the [Rhine–Alpine Corridor](/wiki/Rhine%E2%80%93Alpine_Corridor "Rhine–Alpine Corridor"), the Atlantic Corridor, the [North Sea–Mediterranean Corridor](/wiki/North_Sea%E2%80%93Mediterranean_Corridor "North Sea–Mediterranean Corridor"), and the [Rhine–Danube Corridor](/wiki/Rhine%E2%80%93Danube_Corridor "Rhine–Danube Corridor"). Road transportation was organised under the TEN-T by the [Trans-European road network](/wiki/Trans-European_road_network "Trans-European road network"). [Bundesautobahn 7](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_7 "Bundesautobahn 7") is the longest national [motorway](/wiki/Motorway "Motorway") in the EU at 963 km (598 mi).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Port_of_Rotterdam_Landsat_8_Photo_8_May_2016.jpg/250px-Port_of_Rotterdam_Landsat_8_Photo_8_May_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Port_of_Rotterdam_Landsat_8_Photo_8_May_2016.jpg)

Satellite photo of the [Port of Rotterdam](/wiki/Port_of_Rotterdam "Port of Rotterdam")

Maritime transportation is organised under the TEN-T by the [Trans-European Inland Waterway network](/wiki/Trans-European_Inland_Waterway_network "Trans-European Inland Waterway network"), and the [Trans-European Seaport network](/wiki/Trans-European_Seaport_network "Trans-European Seaport network"). European [seaports](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport") are categorised as international, community, or regional. The [Port of Rotterdam](/wiki/Port_of_Rotterdam "Port of Rotterdam") is the busiest in the EU, and the world's largest seaport outside of [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), located in and near the city of [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), in the province of [South Holland](/wiki/South_Holland "South Holland") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands").[[217]](#cite_note-238)[[218]](#cite_note-239) The [European Maritime Safety Agency](/wiki/European_Maritime_Safety_Agency "European Maritime Safety Agency") (EMSA), founded in 2002 in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), Portugal, is charged with reducing the risk of [maritime accidents](/wiki/Shipwreck "Shipwreck"), [marine pollution](/wiki/Marine_pollution "Marine pollution") from ships and the [loss of human lives at sea](/wiki/Search_and_rescue "Search and rescue") by helping to enforce the pertinent EU legislation.

Air transportation is organised under the TEN-T by the [Trans-European Airport network](/wiki/Trans-European_Airport_network "Trans-European Airport network"). European [airports](/wiki/Airport "Airport") are categorised as international, community, or regional. The [Charles de Gaulle Airport](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle_Airport "Charles de Gaulle Airport") is the busiest in the EU, located in and near the city of [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), in France.[[219]](#cite_note-:FR2021-240) The [European Common Aviation Area](/wiki/European_Common_Aviation_Area "European Common Aviation Area") (ECAA) is a [single market](/wiki/Single_market "Single market") in [aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation"). ECAA agreements were signed on 5 May 2006 in [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg"), Austria between the EU and some third countries. The ECAA liberalises the air transport industry by allowing any company from any ECAA member state to fly between any ECAA member states airports, thereby allowing a "foreign" airline to provide domestic flights. The [Single European Sky](/wiki/Single_European_Sky "Single European Sky") (SES) is an initiative that seeks to reform the European [air traffic management](/wiki/Air_traffic_management "Air traffic management") system through a series of actions carried out in four different levels (institutional, operational, technological and control and supervision) with the aim of satisfying the needs of the European airspace in terms of capacity, safety, efficiency and environmental impact. Civil [aviation safety](/wiki/Aviation_safety "Aviation safety") is under the responsibility of the [European Union Aviation Safety Agency](/wiki/European_Union_Aviation_Safety_Agency "European Union Aviation Safety Agency") (EASA). It carries out [certification](/wiki/Type_certificate "Type certificate"), regulation and standardisation and also performs investigation and monitoring. The idea of a European-level aviation safety authority goes back to 1996, but the agency was only legally established in 2002, and began operating in 2003.

Rail transportation is organised under the TEN-T by the [Trans-European Rail network](/wiki/Trans-European_Rail_network "Trans-European Rail network"), made up of the [high-speed rail network](/wiki/Trans-European_high-speed_rail_network "Trans-European high-speed rail network") and the [conventional rail network](/wiki/Trans-European_conventional_rail_network "Trans-European conventional rail network"). The [Gare du Nord](/wiki/Gare_du_Nord "Gare du Nord") railway station is the busiest in the EU, located in and near the city of [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), in France.[[220]](#cite_note-SNCF-241)[[221]](#cite_note-RATP-242) [Rail transport in Europe](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Europe "Rail transport in Europe") is being synchronised with the [European Rail Traffic Management System](/wiki/European_Rail_Traffic_Management_System "European Rail Traffic Management System") (ERTMS) with the goal of greatly enhancing safety, increase efficiency of train transports and enhance cross-border interoperability. This is done by replacing former national [signalling](/wiki/Railway_signal "Railway signal") equipment and operational procedures with a single new Europe-wide standard for train control and command systems. This system is conducted by the [European Union Agency for Railways](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_Railways "European Union Agency for Railways") (ERA).
