#### Schengen Area

Main article: [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Map_of_the_Schengen_Area.svg/250px-Map_of_the_Schengen_Area.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Schengen_Area.svg)

Map of the Schengen Area

Schengen Area

Countries *de facto* participating

Members of the EU committed by treaty to join the Schengen Area in the future

The Schengen Area is an area comprising 29 European countries that have officially abolished all passport and all other types of [border control](/wiki/Border_control "Border control") at their mutual borders. Being an element within the wider [area of freedom, security and justice](/wiki/Area_of_freedom,_security_and_justice "Area of freedom, security and justice") policy of the EU, it mostly functions as a single jurisdiction under [a common visa policy](/wiki/Visa_policies_in_the_European_Union "Visa policies in the European Union") for international travel purposes. The area is named after the 1985 [Schengen Agreement](/wiki/Schengen_Agreement "Schengen Agreement") and the 1990 [Schengen Convention](/wiki/Schengen_Convention "Schengen Convention"), both signed in [Schengen, Luxembourg](/wiki/Schengen,_Luxembourg "Schengen, Luxembourg"). Of the 27 EU member states, 25 participate in the Schengen Area. Of the EU members that are not part of the Schengen Area, one—[Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_and_the_European_Union "Cyprus and the European Union")—is legally obligated to join the area in the future; [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_and_the_European_Union "Ireland and the European Union") maintains an [opt-out](/wiki/Opt-outs_in_the_European_Union "Opt-outs in the European Union"), and instead operates [its own visa policy](/wiki/Visa_policy_of_Ireland "Visa policy of Ireland"). The four [European Free Trade Association](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association") (EFTA) member states, [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland%E2%80%93European_Union_relations "Iceland–European Union relations"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein-European_Union_relations "Liechtenstein-European Union relations"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway-European_Union_relations "Norway-European Union relations"), and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland-European_Union_relations "Switzerland-European Union relations"), are not members of the EU, but have signed agreements in association with the Schengen Agreement. Also, three [European microstates](/wiki/European_microstates "European microstates")—[Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino") and the [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City")—maintain open borders for passenger traffic with their neighbours, and are therefore considered de facto members of the Schengen Area due to the practical impossibility of travelling to or from them without transiting through at least one Schengen member country.

### Electronic communications and space

Main articles: [Telecommunications in the European Union](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_the_European_Union "Telecommunications in the European Union") and [European Union Space Programme](/wiki/European_Union_Space_Programme "European Union Space Programme")

Further information: [European Union roaming regulations](/wiki/European_Union_roaming_regulations "European Union roaming regulations")

See also: [Agency for Support for BEREC](/wiki/Agency_for_Support_for_BEREC "Agency for Support for BEREC"), [European Union Agency for the Space Programme](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_the_Space_Programme "European Union Agency for the Space Programme"), and [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency")

Mobile communication [roaming charges](/wiki/European_Union_roaming_regulations "European Union roaming regulations") are abolished throughout the EU, Iceland, Liechtenstein and Norway.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Galileo_Control_Centre_at_the_DLR_Oberpfaffenhofen_site.jpg/250px-Galileo_Control_Centre_at_the_DLR_Oberpfaffenhofen_site.jpg)](/wiki/File:Galileo_Control_Centre_at_the_DLR_Oberpfaffenhofen_site.jpg)

[Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") control centre in [Oberpfaffenhofen](/wiki/Oberpfaffenhofen "Oberpfaffenhofen"), Germany

The [European Union Agency for the Space Programme](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_the_Space_Programme "European Union Agency for the Space Programme") (EUSPA), headquartered in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), Czech Republic, was established in 2021 to manage the [European Union Space Programme](/wiki/European_Union_Space_Programme "European Union Space Programme") in order to implement the pre-existing *European Space Policy*, established on 22 May 2007 between the EU and the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") (ESA), known collectively as the *European Space Council*. This was the first common political framework for space activities established by the EU. Each member state has pursued to some extent their own national space policy, though often co-ordinating through the ESA. [Günter Verheugen](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Verheugen "Günter Verheugen"), the [European Commissioner for Enterprise and Industry](/wiki/European_Commissioner_for_Enterprise_and_Industry "European Commissioner for Enterprise and Industry"), has stated that even though the EU is "a world leader in the technology", it was "being put on the defensive by the United States and Russia and that it only has about a 10-year technological advantage on China and India, which are racing to catch up".

[Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") is a [global navigation satellite system](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") (GNSS) that went live in 2016, created by the EU through the ESA, operated by the EUSPA, with two ground operations centres in [Fucino](/wiki/Fucine_Lake "Fucine Lake"), Italy, and [Oberpfaffenhofen](/wiki/Oberpfaffenhofen "Oberpfaffenhofen"), Germany. The €10 billion project is named after the Italian astronomer [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei"). One of the aims of Galileo is to provide an independent high-precision positioning system so European political and military authorities do not have to rely on the US [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System"), or the Russian [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS") systems, which could be disabled or degraded by their operators at any time. The [European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service](/wiki/European_Geostationary_Navigation_Overlay_Service "European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service") (EGNOS) is a [satellite-based augmentation system](/wiki/Satellite-based_augmentation_system "Satellite-based augmentation system") (SBAS) developed by the ESA and [EUROCONTROL](/wiki/EUROCONTROL "EUROCONTROL"). Currently, it supplements the GPS by reporting on the reliability and accuracy of their positioning data and sending out corrections. The system will supplement Galileo in a future version. The [Copernicus Programme](/wiki/Copernicus_Programme "Copernicus Programme") is the EU's Earth observation programme coordinated and managed by EUSPA in partnership with ESA. It aims at achieving a global, continuous, autonomous, high quality, wide range Earth observation capacity, providing accurate, timely and easily accessible information to, among other things, improve the management of the environment, understand and [mitigate the effects of climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation"), and ensure civil security.

### Agriculture and fisheries

Main articles: [Common Agricultural Policy](/wiki/Common_Agricultural_Policy "Common Agricultural Policy") and [Common Fisheries Policy](/wiki/Common_Fisheries_Policy "Common Fisheries Policy")

See also: [European Fisheries Control Agency](/wiki/European_Fisheries_Control_Agency "European Fisheries Control Agency") and [European Food Safety Authority](/wiki/European_Food_Safety_Authority "European Food Safety Authority")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/European_Union_Exclusive_Economic_Zones.PNG/250px-European_Union_Exclusive_Economic_Zones.PNG)](/wiki/File:European_Union_Exclusive_Economic_Zones.PNG)

The EU's [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone") (EEZ). At 25 million square kilometres, it is the largest in the world.[[222]](#cite_note-243)

The Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) is the [agricultural policy](/wiki/Agricultural_policy "Agricultural policy") of the European Union. It implements a system of [agricultural subsidies](/wiki/Agricultural_subsidies "Agricultural subsidies") and other programmes. It was introduced in 1962 and has since then undergone several changes to reduce the EEC budget cost (from 73% in 1985 to 37% in 2017) and consider rural development in its aims. It has, however, been criticised on the grounds of its cost and its environmental and humanitarian effects.

Likewise, the Common Fisheries Policy (CFP) is the [fisheries](/wiki/Fisheries "Fisheries") policy of the European Union. It sets quotas for which member states are allowed to catch each type of fish, as well as encouraging the [fishing industry](/wiki/Fishing_industry "Fishing industry") by various market interventions and [fishing subsidies](/wiki/Fishing_subsidies "Fishing subsidies"). It was introduced in 2009 with the Treaty of Lisbon, which formally enshrined fisheries conservation policy as one of the handful of "exclusive competences" reserved for the European Union.

### Labour

See also: [European Agency for Safety and Health at Work](/wiki/European_Agency_for_Safety_and_Health_at_Work "European Agency for Safety and Health at Work"), [European Centre for the Development of Vocational Training](/wiki/European_Centre_for_the_Development_of_Vocational_Training "European Centre for the Development of Vocational Training"), [European Foundation for the Improvement of Living and Working Conditions](/wiki/European_Foundation_for_the_Improvement_of_Living_and_Working_Conditions "European Foundation for the Improvement of Living and Working Conditions"), [European Labour Authority](/wiki/European_Labour_Authority "European Labour Authority"), [European Training Foundation](/wiki/European_Training_Foundation "European Training Foundation"), and [EURES](/wiki/EURES "EURES")

The free movement of persons means that [EU citizens](/wiki/Citizenship_of_the_European_Union "Citizenship of the European Union") can move freely between member states to live, work, study or retire in another country. This required the lowering of administrative formalities and recognition of professional qualifications of other states.[[223]](#cite_note-Europa_Single_Market_P-244) The EU seasonally adjusted unemployment rate was 6.7 per cent in September 2018.[[224]](#cite_note-unemployment-245) The euro area unemployment rate was 8.1 per cent.[[224]](#cite_note-unemployment-245) Among the member states, the lowest unemployment rates were recorded in the Czech Republic (2.3 per cent), Germany and Poland (both 3.4 per cent), and the highest in Spain (10.61 per cent in 2024)[[225]](#cite_note-246) and Greece (9.6 per cent in November 2024).[[226]](#cite_note-247) The European Union has long sought to mitigate the effects of free markets by protecting workers' rights and preventing [social](/wiki/Social_dumping "Social dumping") and [environmental dumping](/wiki/Environmental_dumping "Environmental dumping").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] To this end, it has adopted laws establishing minimum employment and environmental standards. These included the [Working Time Directive](/wiki/Working_Time_Directive "Working Time Directive") and the [Environmental Impact Assessment Directive](/wiki/Environmental_Impact_Assessment_Directive_2011 "Environmental Impact Assessment Directive 2011"). The European Directive about Minimum Wage, which looks to lift minimum wages and strengthen collective bargaining was approved by the European Parliament in September 2022.[[227]](#cite_note-248)

### Regional development

Main article: [Regional policy of the European Union](/wiki/Regional_policy_of_the_European_Union "Regional policy of the European Union")

See also: [European Committee of the Regions](/wiki/European_Committee_of_the_Regions "European Committee of the Regions") and [European Investment Bank](/wiki/European_Investment_Bank "European Investment Bank")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/European_regional_policy_2021.svg/250px-European_regional_policy_2021.svg.png)](/wiki/File:European_regional_policy_2021.svg)

The five [European Structural and Investment Funds](/wiki/European_Structural_and_Investment_Funds "European Structural and Investment Funds") are supporting the development of the EU regions, primarily the underdeveloped ones, located mostly in the states of [central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and southern Europe.[[228]](#cite_note-249)[[229]](#cite_note-Business2000_Funds-250) Another fund (the [Instrument for Pre-Accession Assistance](/wiki/Instrument_for_Pre-Accession_Assistance "Instrument for Pre-Accession Assistance")) provides support for candidate members to transform their country to conform to the EU's standard. Demographic transition to a society of ageing population, low fertility-rates and depopulation of non-metropolitan regions is tackled within this policies.

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of the European Union](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_European_Union "Demographics of the European Union")

See also: [European Union citizenship](/wiki/European_Union_citizenship "European Union citizenship")

### Population

Further information: [List of European Union member states by population](/wiki/List_of_European_Union_member_states_by_population "List of European Union member states by population")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Population_density_by_NUTS_3_region_%282017%29.svg/250px-Population_density_by_NUTS_3_region_%282017%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Population_density_by_NUTS_3_region_(2017).svg)

Map showing the population density by [NUTS3 region](/wiki/Nomenclature_of_Territorial_Units_for_Statistics "Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics"), 2017, including non-EU countries

The population of the EU in 2024 was about 450 million people, corresponding to 5.8 per cent of the world population.[[5]](#cite_note-population-8)[[230]](#cite_note-251) The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") across the EU was 106 inhabitants per square kilometre, which is more than the world average.[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228) It is highest in areas in central and western Europe, sometimes referred to as the "[blue banana](/wiki/Blue_banana "Blue banana")", while [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") in the north are much more sparsely populated. The total population of the EU has been slightly decreasing for several years, contracting by 0.04 per cent in 2021.[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228) This is due to a low birth rate of about 1.5 children per woman, less than the world average of 2.3.[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228) In total, 4.1 million babies were born in the EU in 2021.[[231]](#cite_note-252)

[Immigration to Europe](/wiki/Immigration_to_Europe "Immigration to Europe") partially compensates for the natural population decrease. Of the people residing in the EU, 5.3 per cent are not [EU citizens](/wiki/European_Union_citizenship "European Union citizenship").[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228) There were 31 non-EU citizenships that each accounted for at least 1 per cent of non-EU citizens living in the EU, of which the largest were [Moroccan](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), [Syrian](/wiki/Syria "Syria") and [Chinese](/wiki/China "China").[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228) Around 1.9 million people [immigrated to one of the EU member states](/wiki/Immigration_to_Europe "Immigration to Europe") from a non-EU country during 2020, and a total of 956,000 people emigrated from a member state to go to a non-EU country during the same year.[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228)

#### Urbanisation

See also: [List of cities in the European Union by population within city limits](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_European_Union_by_population_within_city_limits "List of cities in the European Union by population within city limits") and [List of urban areas in the European Union](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_European_Union "List of urban areas in the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/La_Tour_Eiffel_vue_de_la_Tour_Saint-Jacques%2C_Paris_ao%C3%BBt_2014_%282%29.jpg/250px-La_Tour_Eiffel_vue_de_la_Tour_Saint-Jacques%2C_Paris_ao%C3%BBt_2014_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Tour_Eiffel_vue_de_la_Tour_Saint-Jacques,_Paris_ao%C3%BBt_2014_(2).jpg)

The [Paris metropolitan area](/wiki/Paris_metropolitan_area "Paris metropolitan area") is the most populous urban area in the EU.

More than two thirds (68.2%) of EU inhabitants lived in [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") in 2020, which is slightly less than the world average.[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228) Cities are largely spread out across the EU with a large grouping in and around the [Benelux](/wiki/Benelux "Benelux").[[232]](#cite_note-253) The EU contains about 40 urban areas with populations of over 1 million. With a population of over 13 million,[[233]](#cite_note-paris_AAV20_pop-254) [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") is the largest metropolitan area and the only [megacity](/wiki/Megacity "Megacity") in the EU.[[234]](#cite_note-eurostat-255)

Paris is followed by [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), the [Ruhr](/wiki/Ruhr "Ruhr"), [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), and [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), all with a metropolitan population of over 4 million. The EU also has numerous [polycentric](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") urbanised regions like [Rhine-Ruhr](/wiki/Rhine-Ruhr "Rhine-Ruhr") ([Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), [Dortmund](/wiki/Dortmund "Dortmund"), [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf") et al.), [Randstad](/wiki/Randstad "Randstad") ([Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht "Utrecht") et al.), [Frankfurt Rhine-Main](/wiki/Frankfurt_Rhine-Main "Frankfurt Rhine-Main") ([Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), [Wiesbaden](/wiki/Wiesbaden "Wiesbaden"), [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz") et al.), the [Flemish Diamond](/wiki/Flemish_Diamond "Flemish Diamond") ([Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven"), [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") et al.) and [Upper Silesian-Moravian area](/wiki/Upper_Silesian-Moravian_metropolitan_area "Upper Silesian-Moravian metropolitan area") ([Katowice](/wiki/Katowice "Katowice"), [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava") et al.).[[234]](#cite_note-eurostat-255)

The EU has 24 official languages: [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language"), [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"), [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"), [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"), [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"), [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"), [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"), [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"). Important documents, such as legislation, are translated into every official language and the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") provides translation for documents and plenary sessions.[[237]](#cite_note-Official_Languages-261)[[238]](#cite_note-262) Most EU institutions use only a handful of working languages: the European Commission conducts its internal business in three *procedural languages*: English, French, and German;[[239]](#cite_note-procedural-263) the [Court of Justice](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union") uses French as the working language,[[240]](#cite_note-264) and the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") conducts its business primarily in English.[[241]](#cite_note-265)[[242]](#cite_note-266) Even though [language policy](/wiki/Language_policy "Language policy") is the responsibility of member states, EU institutions promote multilingualism among its citizens.[[j]](#cite_note-art249-144)[[243]](#cite_note-267)

The most widely spoken language in the EU is [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"); the language is spoken by 50 per cent of the population[[236]](#cite_note-EU-Lang-257) and studied by 95 per cent of school students,[[244]](#cite_note-268) although following the [withdrawal of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") only 2% of the population speak it natively.[[236]](#cite_note-EU-Lang-257) [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") are spoken by 29 per cent and 25 per cent of the population. More than half (59 per cent) of EU citizens are able to engage in a conversation in a language other than their mother tongue, and more than a tenth (11%) are quadrilingual.[[236]](#cite_note-EU-Lang-257)

[Luxembourgish](/wiki/Luxembourgish "Luxembourgish") (in Luxembourg) and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language") (in Cyprus) are the only two national languages that are not official languages of the EU. [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") and [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language") are not recognised official languages of the EU but have official status in Spain. Therefore, official translations of the treaties are made into them and citizens have the right to correspond with the institutions in these languages.[[245]](#cite_note-269)[[246]](#cite_note-270) There are about 150 [regional](/wiki/Regional_language "Regional language") and [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") in the EU, spoken by up to 50 million people.[[247]](#cite_note-Many_tongues,_one_family-271) The [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages") ratified by most EU states provides general guidelines that states can follow to protect their linguistic heritage. The [European Day of Languages](/wiki/European_Day_of_Languages "European Day of Languages") is held annually on 26 September and is aimed at encouraging language learning across Europe.[[248]](#cite_note-272)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in the European Union](/wiki/Religion_in_the_European_Union "Religion in the European Union")

Religious affiliation in the EU (2015)[[1]](#cite_note-EB2015-4)

| Affiliation | Per cent of EU population | |
| --- | --- | --- |

| [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") | 45.3 |  |
| [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") | 11.1 |  |
| [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") | 9.6 |  |
| Other Christian | 5.6 |  |

| Other faiths | 2.6 |  |

The EU has no formal connection to any religion. Article 17 of the [Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Functioning_of_the_European_Union "Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union")[[249]](#cite_note-273) recognises the "status under national law of churches and religious associations" as well as that of "philosophical and non-confessional organisations".[[250]](#cite_note-Consolidated_Treaties-274) The preamble to the [Treaty on European Union](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty") mentions the "cultural, religious and humanist inheritance of Europe".[[250]](#cite_note-Consolidated_Treaties-274)[[251]](#cite_note-275) Discussion over the draft texts of the European Constitution and later the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") included proposals to mention [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") or a [god](/wiki/God "God"), or both, in the preamble of the text, but the idea faced opposition and was dropped.[[252]](#cite_note-EUO_Merkel_God-276)

Christians in the EU include [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") of both [Latin](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") and [Eastern Rite](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), numerous [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") denominations with [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans"), and [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") forming the majority of Protestant affiliations, and the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"). In 2009, the EU had an estimated [Muslim population](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Muslim_population "List of countries by Muslim population") of 13 million,[[253]](#cite_note-mgmpPRC-277) and an estimated [Jewish population](/wiki/Jewish_population "Jewish population") of over a million.[[254]](#cite_note-278) The other world religions of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), and [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") are also represented in the EU population. [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat")'s [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") opinion polls showed in 2005 that 52 per cent of EU citizens believed in a god, 27 per cent in "some sort of spirit or life force", and 18 per cent had no form of belief.[[255]](#cite_note-Eurostat_Religion-279)

Into the 21st century, many countries have experienced falling church attendance and membership.[[256]](#cite_note-About_SecE-280) The countries where the fewest people reported a religious belief were Estonia (16 per cent) and the Czech Republic (19 per cent).[[255]](#cite_note-Eurostat_Religion-279) The most religious countries were Malta (95 per cent, predominantly Catholic) as well as Cyprus and Romania (both predominantly Orthodox) each with about 90 per cent of citizens professing a belief in God. Across the EU, belief was higher among women, older people, those with religious upbringing, those who left school at 15 or 16, and those "positioning themselves on the right of the political scale".[[255]](#cite_note-Eurostat_Religion-279)

### Education and research

Main articles: [Educational policies and initiatives of the European Union](/wiki/Educational_policies_and_initiatives_of_the_European_Union "Educational policies and initiatives of the European Union") and [Framework Programmes for Research and Technological Development](/wiki/Framework_Programmes_for_Research_and_Technological_Development "Framework Programmes for Research and Technological Development")

See also: [European Institute of Innovation and Technology](/wiki/European_Institute_of_Innovation_and_Technology "European Institute of Innovation and Technology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Holbein-erasmus.jpg/250px-Holbein-erasmus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holbein-erasmus.jpg)

[Erasmus of Rotterdam](/wiki/Erasmus "Erasmus"), the [Renaissance humanist](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") after whom the [Erasmus Programme](/wiki/Erasmus_Programme "Erasmus Programme") is named

Basic education is an area where the EU's role is limited to supporting national governments. In higher education, the policy was developed in the 1980s in programmes supporting exchanges and mobility. The most visible of these has been the [Erasmus Programme](/wiki/Erasmus_Programme "Erasmus Programme"), a university exchange programme which began in 1987. In its first 20 years, it supported international exchange opportunities for well over 1.5 million university and college students and became a symbol of European student life.[[257]](#cite_note-281)

There are similar programmes for school pupils and teachers, for trainees in [vocational education and training](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education"), and for adult learners in the [Lifelong Learning Programme 2007–2013](/wiki/Lifelong_Learning_Programme_2007%E2%80%932013 "Lifelong Learning Programme 2007–2013"). These programmes are designed to encourage a wider knowledge of other countries and to spread good practices in the education and training fields across the EU.[[258]](#cite_note-282)[[259]](#cite_note-283) Through its support of the [Bologna Process](/wiki/Bologna_Process "Bologna Process"), the EU is supporting comparable standards and compatible degrees across Europe. Scientific development is facilitated through the EU's [Framework Programmes](/wiki/Framework_Programmes_for_Research_and_Technological_Development "Framework Programmes for Research and Technological Development"), the first of which started in 1984. The aims of EU policy in this area are to co-ordinate and stimulate research. The independent [European Research Council](/wiki/European_Research_Council "European Research Council") allocates EU funds to European or national research projects.[[260]](#cite_note-284) EU [research and technological framework programmes](/wiki/Framework_Programmes_for_Research_and_Technological_Development "Framework Programmes for Research and Technological Development") deal in a number of areas, for example energy where the aim is to develop a diverse mix of [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") to help the environment and to reduce dependence on imported fuels.[[261]](#cite_note-285)

### Health

Main article: [Healthcare in Europe](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Europe "Healthcare in Europe")

See also: [European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control](/wiki/European_Centre_for_Disease_Prevention_and_Control "European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control"), [European Chemicals Agency](/wiki/European_Chemicals_Agency "European Chemicals Agency"), [European Medicines Agency](/wiki/European_Medicines_Agency "European Medicines Agency"), and [European Monitoring Centre for Drugs and Drug Addiction](/wiki/European_Monitoring_Centre_for_Drugs_and_Drug_Addiction "European Monitoring Centre for Drugs and Drug Addiction")

Article 35 of the [Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights_of_the_European_Union "Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union") affirms that "A high level of human health protection shall be ensured in the definition and implementation of all Union policies and activities". The [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission")'s [Directorate-General for Health and Consumers](/wiki/Directorate-General_for_Health_and_Consumers_(European_Commission) "Directorate-General for Health and Consumers (European Commission)") seeks to align national laws on the protection of people's health, on the consumers' rights, on the safety of food and other products.[[262]](#cite_note-286)[[263]](#cite_note-287)[[264]](#cite_note-288)

All EU and many other European countries offer their citizens a free [European Health Insurance Card](/wiki/European_Health_Insurance_Card "European Health Insurance Card") which, on a reciprocal basis, provides insurance for emergency medical treatment insurance when visiting other participating European countries.[[265]](#cite_note-289) A directive on cross-border healthcare aims at promoting co-operation on health care between member states and facilitating access to safe and high-quality cross-border healthcare for European patients.[[266]](#cite_note-290)[[267]](#cite_note-291)[[268]](#cite_note-292)

The life expectancy in the EU was 80.1 year at birth in 2021, among the highest in the world and around nine years higher than the world average.[[207]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-228) In general, life expectancy is lower in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") than in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe").[[269]](#cite_note-293) In 2018, the EU region with the highest life expectancy was [Madrid](/wiki/Comunidad_de_Madrid "Comunidad de Madrid"), Spain at 85.2 years, followed by the Spanish regions of [La Rioja](/wiki/La_Rioja_(Spain) "La Rioja (Spain)") and [Castilla y León](/wiki/Castilla_y_Le%C3%B3n "Castilla y León") both at 84.3 years, [Trentino](/wiki/Trentino "Trentino") in Italy at 84.3 years and [Île-de-France](/wiki/%C3%8Ele-de-France "Île-de-France") in France at 84.2 years.[[270]](#cite_note-294)

### Social rights and equality

Main articles: [European social model](/wiki/European_social_model "European social model"), [European Social Fund Plus](/wiki/European_Social_Fund_Plus "European Social Fund Plus"), [Welfare State](/wiki/Welfare_State "Welfare State"), [European Social Charter](/wiki/European_Social_Charter "European Social Charter"), [European Voluntary Service](/wiki/European_Voluntary_Service "European Voluntary Service"), [European labour law](/wiki/European_labour_law "European labour law"), [List of countries by guaranteed minimum income](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_guaranteed_minimum_income "List of countries by guaranteed minimum income"), [European Pillar of Social Rights](/wiki/European_Pillar_of_Social_Rights "European Pillar of Social Rights"), and [List of European Union member states by minimum wage](/wiki/List_of_European_Union_member_states_by_minimum_wage "List of European Union member states by minimum wage")

The EU has also sought to coordinate the social security and health systems of member states to facilitate individuals exercising free movement rights and to ensure they maintain their ability to access social security and health services in other member states. Since 2019 there has been a European commissioner for equality and the [European Institute for Gender Equality](/wiki/European_Institute_for_Gender_Equality "European Institute for Gender Equality") has existed since 2007. A Directive on countering gender-based violence has been proposed.[[271]](#cite_note-295)[[272]](#cite_note-296) In September 2022, a European Care strategy was approved in order to provide "quality, affordable and accessible care services".[[273]](#cite_note-297) The [European Social Charter](/wiki/European_Social_Charter "European Social Charter") is the main body that recognises the social rights of European citizens. In 2020, the first ever European Union Strategy on LGBTIQ equality was approved under [Helena Dalli](/wiki/Helena_Dalli "Helena Dalli") mandate.[[274]](#cite_note-298) In December 2021, the commission announced the intention of codifying a union-wide law against LGBT hate crimes.[[275]](#cite_note-299)

### Freedom, security and justice

Main article: [Area of freedom, security and justice](/wiki/Area_of_freedom,_security_and_justice "Area of freedom, security and justice")

Further information: [Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights_of_the_European_Union "Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union")

See also: [eu-LISA](/wiki/Eu-LISA "Eu-LISA"), [Eurojust](/wiki/Eurojust "Eurojust"), [European Institute for Gender Equality](/wiki/European_Institute_for_Gender_Equality "European Institute for Gender Equality"), [European Union Agency for Asylum](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_Asylum "European Union Agency for Asylum"), [European Union Agency for Cybersecurity](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_Cybersecurity "European Union Agency for Cybersecurity"), [European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Training](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_Law_Enforcement_Training "European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Training"), [Europol](/wiki/Europol "Europol"), [Frontex](/wiki/Frontex "Frontex"), and [Fundamental Rights Agency](/wiki/Fundamental_Rights_Agency "Fundamental Rights Agency")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/04CFREU-Article2-Crop.jpg/250px-04CFREU-Article2-Crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:04CFREU-Article2-Crop.jpg)

The [Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights_of_the_European_Union "Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union") contains a wide range of political, social, and economic rights for EU citizens.

Since the creation of the European Union in 1993, it has developed its competencies in the area of justice and home affairs; initially at an intergovernmental level and later by supranationalism. Accordingly, the union has legislated in areas such as [extradition](/wiki/European_Arrest_Warrant "European Arrest Warrant"),[[276]](#cite_note-300) family law,[[277]](#cite_note-301) asylum law,[[278]](#cite_note-302) and criminal justice.[[279]](#cite_note-303)

The EU has also established agencies to co-ordinate police, prosecution and civil litigations across the member states: [Europol](/wiki/Europol "Europol") for police co-operation, [CEPOL](/wiki/CEPOL "CEPOL") for training of police forces[[280]](#cite_note-304) and the [Eurojust](/wiki/Eurojust "Eurojust") for co-operation between prosecutors and courts.[[281]](#cite_note-305) It also operates the [EUCARIS](/wiki/EUCARIS "EUCARIS") database of vehicles and drivers, the [Eurodac](/wiki/Eurodac "Eurodac"), the [European Criminal Records Information System](/wiki/European_Criminal_Records_Information_System "European Criminal Records Information System"), the [European Cybercrime Centre](/wiki/European_Cybercrime_Centre "European Cybercrime Centre"), [FADO](/wiki/FADO "FADO"), [PRADO](/wiki/Public_Register_of_Travel_and_Identity_Documents_Online "Public Register of Travel and Identity Documents Online") and others.

Prohibitions against discrimination have a long standing in the treaties. In more recent years, these have been supplemented by powers to legislate against discrimination based on race, religion, disability, age, and sexual orientation.[[s]](#cite_note-art2(7)_Amsterdam-306) The treaties declare that the European Union itself is "founded on the values of respect for [human dignity](/wiki/Human_dignity "Human dignity"), [freedom](/wiki/Liberty "Liberty"), democracy, [equality](/wiki/Equality_before_the_law "Equality before the law"), the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law") and respect for human rights, including the rights of persons belonging to [minorities](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group") ... in a society in which pluralism, non-discrimination, tolerance, justice, solidarity and equality between women and men prevail."[[282]](#cite_note-307) By virtue of these powers, the EU has enacted legislation on [sexism](/wiki/Sexism "Sexism") in the work-place, [age discrimination](/wiki/Ageism "Ageism"), and [racial discrimination](/wiki/Racism "Racism").[[t]](#cite_note-308)

In 2009, the [Lisbon Treaty](/wiki/Lisbon_Treaty "Lisbon Treaty") gave legal effect to the [Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights_of_the_European_Union "Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union"). The charter is a codified catalogue of [fundamental rights](/wiki/Fundamental_right "Fundamental right") against which the EU's legal acts can be judged. It consolidates many rights which were previously recognised by the Court of Justice and derived from the "constitutional traditions common to the member states".[[283]](#cite_note-309) The Court of Justice has long recognised fundamental rights and has, on occasion, invalidated EU legislation based on its failure to adhere to those fundamental rights.[[284]](#cite_note-europarl-rights-310) Signing the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights") (ECHR) is a condition for EU membership.[[u]](#cite_note-311) Previously, the EU itself could not accede to the convention as it is neither a state[[v]](#cite_note-312) nor had the competence to accede.[[w]](#cite_note-313) The Lisbon Treaty and Protocol 14 to the ECHR have changed this: the former binds the EU to accede to the convention while the latter formally permits it.

The EU is independent from the Council of Europe, although they share purpose and ideas, especially on the rule of law, human rights and democracy. Furthermore, the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights") and [European Social Charter](/wiki/European_Social_Charter "European Social Charter"), as well as the [source of law](/wiki/Source_of_law "Source of law") for the [Charter of Fundamental Rights](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights_of_the_European_Union "Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union") are created by the Council of Europe. The EU has also promoted human rights issues in the wider world. The EU opposes the death penalty and has proposed its worldwide abolition. Abolition of the death penalty is a condition for EU membership.[[285]](#cite_note-314) On 19 October 2020, the European Union revealed new plans to create a legal structure to act against [human rights violations](/wiki/Human_rights_violations "Human rights violations") worldwide. The new plan was expected to provide the European Union with greater flexibility to target and sanction those responsible for serious human rights violations and abuses around the world.[[286]](#cite_note-315)

## Culture

Main article: [Cultural policies of the European Union](/wiki/Cultural_policies_of_the_European_Union "Cultural policies of the European Union")

Cultural co-operation between member states has been an interest of the European Union since its inclusion as a community competency in the Maastricht Treaty.[[287]](#cite_note-Bozoki-316) Actions taken in the cultural area by the EU include the [Culture 2000](/wiki/Culture_2000 "Culture 2000") seven-year programme,[[287]](#cite_note-Bozoki-316) the [European Cultural Month](/wiki/European_Cultural_Month "European Cultural Month") event,[[288]](#cite_note-Culture_Month-317) and orchestras such as the [European Union Youth Orchestra](/wiki/European_Union_Youth_Orchestra "European Union Youth Orchestra").[[289]](#cite_note-318) The [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European_Capital_of_Culture "European Capital of Culture") programme selects one or more cities in every year to assist the [cultural development](/wiki/Sociocultural_evolution "Sociocultural evolution") of that city.[[290]](#cite_note-Capital_Culture-319)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport policies of the European Union](/wiki/Sport_policies_of_the_European_Union "Sport policies of the European Union")

Sport is mainly the responsibility of the member states or other international organisations, rather than of the EU. There are some EU policies that have affected sport, such as the free movement of workers, which was at the core of the [Bosman ruling](/wiki/Bosman_ruling "Bosman ruling") that prohibited national football leagues from imposing quotas on foreign players with EU member state citizenship.[[291]](#cite_note-BBC_Boseman-320) The [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") requires any application of economic rules to take into account the specific nature of sport and its structures based on voluntary activity.[[292]](#cite_note-321) This followed lobbying by governing organisations such as the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"), due to objections over the application of free market principles to sport, which led to an increasing gap between rich and poor clubs.[[293]](#cite_note-IHT_RT-322) The EU does fund a programme for Israeli, Jordanian, Irish, and British football coaches, as part of the Football 4 Peace project.[[294]](#cite_note-323)

### Symbols

Further information: [European Heritage Label](/wiki/European_Heritage_Label "European Heritage Label")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Europa_copy.jpg/250px-Europa_copy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europa_copy.jpg)

[Europa](/wiki/Europa_(consort_of_Zeus) "Europa (consort of Zeus)") and the Bull on a Greek vase, c. 480 BC. [Tarquinia National Museum](/wiki/Tarquinia_National_Museum "Tarquinia National Museum"), Italy

The [flag of Europe](/wiki/Flag_of_Europe "Flag of Europe") consists of a [circle of](/wiki/Circle_of_stars "Circle of stars") 12 golden stars on a blue background. Originally designed in 1955 for the Council of Europe, the flag was adopted by the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities"), the predecessors of the present European Union, in 1986. The Council of Europe gave the flag a symbolic description in the following terms,[[295]](#cite_note-36th-324) although the official symbolic description adopted by the EU omits the reference to the "Western world".[[296]](#cite_note-1996guide-325)

> Against the blue sky of the Western world, the stars symbolise the peoples of Europe in a form of a circle, the sign of union. The number of stars is invariably [twelve](/wiki/12_(number)#Religion "12 (number)"), the figure twelve being the symbol of perfection and entirety.

— Council of Europe. Paris, 7–9 December 1955.

*[United in Diversity](/wiki/Motto_of_the_European_Union "Motto of the European Union")* was adopted as the motto of the union in 2000, having been selected from [proposals](/wiki/A_motto_for_Europe "A motto for Europe") submitted by school pupils.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimons2002110-326) Since 1985, the [flag day](/wiki/Flag_day "Flag day") of the union has been [Europe Day](/wiki/Europe_Day "Europe Day"), on 9 May (the date of the 1950 [Schuman declaration](/wiki/Schuman_declaration "Schuman declaration")). The [anthem of the EU](/wiki/Anthem_of_Europe "Anthem of Europe") is an instrumental version of the prelude to the *[Ode to Joy](/wiki/Ode_to_Joy "Ode to Joy")*, the 4th movement of [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven")'s [ninth symphony](/wiki/Symphony_No._9_(Beethoven) "Symphony No. 9 (Beethoven)"). The anthem was adopted by European Community leaders in 1985 and has since been played on official occasions.[[298]](#cite_note-327)
Besides naming the continent, the [Greek mythological](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology") figure of [Europa](/wiki/Europa_(consort_of_Zeus) "Europa (consort of Zeus)") has frequently been employed as a [personification](/wiki/National_personification "National personification") of Europe. Known from the myth in which [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus") seduces her in the guise of a white bull, Europa has also been referred to in relation to the present union. Statues of Europa and the bull decorate several of the EU's institutions and a portrait of her is seen on the 2013 series of [euro banknotes](/wiki/Euro_banknotes "Euro banknotes"). The bull is, for its part, depicted on all residence permit cards.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemey2007387-328)

[Charles the Great](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), also known as Charlemagne ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Carolus Magnus*) and later recognised as *Pater Europae* ("Father of Europe"),[[300]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-329)[[301]](#cite_note-330)[[302]](#cite_note-331) has a symbolic relevance to Europe. The commission has named [one of its central buildings](/wiki/Charlemagne_building "Charlemagne building") in Brussels after Charlemagne and the city of [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen") has since 1949 awarded the [Charlemagne Prize](/wiki/Charlemagne_Prize "Charlemagne Prize") to champions of European unification.[[303]](#cite_note-332) Since 2008, the organisers of this prize, in conjunction with the European Parliament, have awarded the [Charlemagne Youth Prize](/wiki/European_Charlemagne_Youth_Prize "European Charlemagne Youth Prize") in recognition of similar efforts led by young people.[[304]](#cite_note-333)

### Media

Main articles: [Media freedom in the European Union](/wiki/Media_freedom_in_the_European_Union "Media freedom in the European Union") and [European Broadcasting Union](/wiki/European_Broadcasting_Union "European Broadcasting Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/78/Si%C3%A8ge_d%27Euronews.jpg/250px-Si%C3%A8ge_d%27Euronews.jpg)](/wiki/File:Si%C3%A8ge_d%27Euronews.jpg)

[Euronews](/wiki/Euronews "Euronews") headquarters in [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"), France

[Media freedom](/wiki/Media_freedom "Media freedom") is a [fundamental right](/wiki/Fundamental_rights "Fundamental rights") that applies to all [member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") of the European Union and its [citizens](/wiki/EU_citizens "EU citizens"), as defined in the [EU Charter of Fundamental Rights](/wiki/EU_Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights "EU Charter of Fundamental Rights") as well as the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights").[[305]](#cite_note-eprs-334): 1 Within the [EU enlargement](/wiki/EU_enlargement "EU enlargement") process, guaranteeing media freedom is named a "key indicator of a country's readiness to become part of the EU".[[306]](#cite_note-335)

The majority of media in the European Union are national-orientated, although some EU-wide media focusing on European affairs have emerged since the early 1990s, such as [Euronews](/wiki/Euronews "Euronews"), [Eurosport](/wiki/Eurosport "Eurosport"), [EUobserver](/wiki/EUobserver "EUobserver"), [EURACTIV](/wiki/EURACTIV "EURACTIV") or [Politico Europe](/wiki/Politico_Europe "Politico Europe").[[307]](#cite_note-336)[[308]](#cite_note-337) [Arte](/wiki/Arte "Arte") is a public Franco-German TV network that promotes programming in the areas of culture and the arts. 80 per cent of its programming are provided in equal proportion by the two member companies, while the remainder is being provided by the [European Economic Interest Grouping](/wiki/European_Economic_Interest_Grouping "European Economic Interest Grouping") *ARTE GEIE* and the channel's European partners.[[309]](#cite_note-338) The [MEDIA Programme](/wiki/MEDIA_Programme "MEDIA Programme") of the European Union has supported the European popular film and audiovisual industries since 1991. It provides support for the development, promotion and distribution of European works within Europe and beyond.[[310]](#cite_note-339)

### Influence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Eiffel_Tower_wearing_Europe_colors_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Eiffel_Tower_wearing_Europe_colors_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eiffel_Tower_wearing_Europe_colors_-_panoramio.jpg)

The [European emblem](/wiki/Flag_of_Europe "Flag of Europe") emblazoned on the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower")

The European Union has had a significant positive economic effect on most member states.[[311]](#cite_note-JoME-340) According to a 2019 study of the member states who joined from 1973 to 2004, "without European integration, per capita incomes would have been, on average, approximately 10% lower in the first ten years after joining the EU".[[311]](#cite_note-JoME-340) Greece was the exception reported by the study, which analysed up to 2008, "to avoid confounding effects from the global financial crisis".[[311]](#cite_note-JoME-340) A 2021 study in the *Journal of Political Economy* found that the 2004 enlargement had aggregate beneficial economic effects on all groups in both the old and new member states. The largest winners were the new member states, in particular unskilled labour in the new member states.[[312]](#cite_note-341)

The European Union is frequently cited as having made a major contribution to peace in Europe, in particular by pacifying border disputes,[[313]](#cite_note-342)[[314]](#cite_note-343) and to the spread of democracy, especially by encouraging democratic reforms in aspiring Eastern European member states after the collapse of the USSR.[[315]](#cite_note-344)[[316]](#cite_note-Risse-2009-345) Scholar [Thomas Risse](/wiki/Thomas_Risse "Thomas Risse") wrote in 2009, "there is a consensus in the literature on Eastern Europe that the EU membership perspective had a huge anchoring effects for the new democracies."[[316]](#cite_note-Risse-2009-345) However, [R. Daniel Kelemen](/wiki/R._Daniel_Kelemen "R. Daniel Kelemen") argues that the EU has proved beneficial to leaders who are overseeing [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding"), as the EU is reluctant to intervene in domestic politics, gives authoritarian governments funds which they can use to strengthen their regimes, and because [freedom of movement within the EU](/wiki/European_single_market#People "European single market") allows dissenting citizens to leave their backsliding countries. At the same time, the union might provide through [Article 7 of the Treaty on European Union](/wiki/Article_7_of_the_Treaty_on_European_Union "Article 7 of the Treaty on European Union") an external constraint that prevents [electoral autocracies](/wiki/Electoral_autocracy "Electoral autocracy"), currently Hungary,[[317]](#cite_note-346) from progressing into [closed autocracies](/wiki/Autocracy "Autocracy").[[318]](#cite_note-347)

## Opt-outs

Main article: [Opt-outs in the European Union](/wiki/Opt-outs_in_the_European_Union "Opt-outs in the European Union")

Three member states have permanent opt-outs from certain aspects of the union:

* Denmark has an opt-out from the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") and the [area of freedom, security and justice](/wiki/Area_of_freedom,_security_and_justice "Area of freedom, security and justice") (AFSJ);
* Ireland has an opt-out of the [Schengen area](/wiki/Schengen_area "Schengen area"), due to its [open border with the UK](/wiki/Common_Travel_Area "Common Travel Area"), and a partial opt-out from the AFSJ
* Poland has a partial opt-out of the [Charter of Fundamental Rights](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights "Charter of Fundamental Rights").
